c_red_shirt_3_saddar_1931_p095.text:
2830-R8
Internal Section Diary dated 16-6-31.
On the evening of 15-6-31 a meeting under the residency of Pazol Rahim Khan of Pazar Kheni was held at panjpir in which about 400 men of Musazai, Sarozai, Masho Khel, Deh Bahadur, Badaber and Hazar Fhani attended, out of which 80 were red shirts and 16 women.
halim Shah of Badaber read out two poems on liberty
Ashiq Shah of Deh Bahadur delivered a speech in which he described the aims of red shirts and requested the public to assist them. He further said that this is all due to the breve deeds of red shirts that Abdul Ghaffar Khan and other political convicts were released before they had finished the terms of their imprisonment.He added that the Govt: is not fulfilling the terms of the truce as political prisoners such as Paii Mohd Amin of Suleman. Khel end of "elekand Agency are still rotting in the jails. At the expiry of the present truce we will set act under instructions. Referring to the repressive laws; he said, that the Committee appointed to enquire into it is not to be relied upon as we dont expect much from it. This province should have the same previleges as are enjoyed by the rest of the Provinces of India. We will sacrifice every thing for the sake of liberty.Referring to the case of the labourers he said that the bungalows are built by the poor labourers but the capitalists enjoy it. He exhorted the public to enlist as red shirts in order to attain liberty and prayed for union. He put in a resolution which was carried out;-
This meeting demands the Government that 60% revenue should he remitted. In case of the produce of grain 40 % would be paid.
In the end he asked the public to pay the revenue at any cost either in shape of cash or rain.

c_red_shirt_3_saddar_1931_p096.text:
Police Department.
Confidential
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 17-6-31.
Peshawar District
A red-shirt meeting was held at Bhanamari at about 5-45 p.m. on 14-6-31. The audience, which numbered about 1000-1200, included about 200-250 red-shirts and 100-150 - women. Sayyid Haider Shah of Bhanamari was the President. Before the actual proceedings started, a blind mullah addressed the audience urging strict adherence to Islamic laws which was the only way to attain liberty.
d
Mullah Khan Mir 'Hilali', President of the Congress Committee, Peshawar City, read out a Pushto poem on the Red-shirt movement, and the Freedom. In this poem he praise red-shirts for their sacrifices in the cause of the freedom of their country, and said that they feared none except God. They were like thorns in the eyes of the Govern- ment although they were maintaining peace in the country. The 'Toadis' were dogs and the red-shirts should let them bark like the dogs who bark on a passing caravan.
Explaining the meaning of the word 'Toady' he remarked that one of the 'Toadies' was the "Civil and Military Gazette" of Lahore, who being a mouthpiece of the Government was always writing against the "Fakhr-i-Sarhad" (Abdul Ghaffar Khan), and the red-shirts.
Hazrat Shah, a Tehkal red-shirts, read out a Pushto poem asking people to wake up and sacrifice their lives for Freedom.
A red-shirt boy read out a Pushto poem praying God to bestow them Liberty.
Mullah Khan Mir got up again and delivered a Pushbo speech describing the services of Abdul Ghaffar Khan, whom he called the "Commander-in-Chief of India", due to whose efforts about 1 lakh red-shirts had been enlisted in the Peshawar District. These red-shirts were preaching people
that

c_red_shirt_3_saddar_1931_p097.text:
Should)
(2)
give up all bad habits and work for Freedom, It was through the good example set by the red-shirts that all' the Pathans were now being praised for their boldness and discipline. Through the efforts of the authorities a party had been formed to oppose the red-shirts which held its meeting at Ziam which proved a failure. The red-clothes sign of
were distinction between the Khudal Khadmatgars and
others. The red-shirts were working for the freedom of the country from the British yoke and for the establishment of a National Government. The newly created "Green-shirts" had been brought into existence by the Government . They 90% daily acted according to the instructions of the (Hon'ble) the Chief Commissioner, and the Deputy Commission er. The red-shirts should offer sacrifices and undergo hardship like Prophet Joseph. Abdul Ghaffar Khan was honour ed for his services to his country. The red-shirts were lovers of freedom, and were enemies of those who were against the freedom of the country. They liked those who were hated by the British, and as the British disliked red- clothes, the Khudai Khidmatgars would like them. Some Maulvis spoke slightly about the red-shirts but the time would come when they would be able distinguish right from the wrong. Whatever the British liked was wrong, and what- ever they disliked, was right. On this the cries of "Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Abdul Ghaffar Khan", etc. were raised. Resuming his speech Khanmir remarked that red-cloth es were not disallowed by the Qoran but they (the red- shirts) did not like to indulge in religious discussions over such things, as they only wanted to do such things as were disliked by the British. He was dealing with the point. politically and not religiously. They wanted some Mullah
a
to.
who should tell them a philtre by which the britsh could be turned out of india. At this time Ibrahim Khan of Bhana- mari said "May God restore Aman-Ulla Khan to his
pand

c_red_shirt_3_saddar_1931_p098.text:
(3)
throne and Khanmir concluded his speach by repeating the same sentence.
Abdul Ghafur Khan, son of Arbab Juma Khan of renkal bala, delivered a speech during which he said that the ob- ject of the Afghan Jirgah was to promote unity amongst the Pathans and to reform their moral condition. The Hindus and the Sikhs were free from such bad habits as existed among the Pathans. A man like Abdul Ghaffar Khan, however, was among the
present a Pathans who went to Jail for the sake of his nation. The British had written in their books that the Pathans were inhuman. Abdul Ghaffar Khan told them to be- come united if they wanted freedom. Now the Pathans had given up violence and were becoming united. The British, who were the enemies of their country, could not see them reformed and so they began sending the Pathans to jail. - The Pathans, had however, proved the strength of their character. They had defeated Government although they were unarmed. They came out of jails before the expiry of their terms of imprisonment. The Government had never regarded them as human beings. They were refused rights equal to the people of the rest of India and besides this laws like the Frontier Crimes Regulation were enforced in their country in addition to the Indian Penal Code. In their Province the rolice had special powers, while the Deputy Commission- er had powers more than the Governors of the other Provinc- es. These powers were provided him by the F.C.R. The Re- form Committee was doing its work, but Mian Ahmad, who was one of its members, had resigned from it as he did not hope that it would support their cause. Due to the efforts of the local and provincial Jirgahs, the Government was prepared to give them something which it had hitherto been refusing to grant. They should form Jirgans everywhere. The Government was subjecting the zemindars with all sorts of oppression. The Khans and Maliks were inwardly against the

c_red_shirt_3_saddar_1931_p099.text:
(4)
T
reduction of the land revenue. Referring to the Assembly hall somb case he remarked that the case against Bhagat Singh etc., had been decided after a trial of about 2 years but Habib Nur. had been hanged after a trial of 24 hours only. They should A raise a common vioce for the cancellation of the Frontie laws. The taxes on tongas and carts were heavy and should be reduced. The Government would accede to their demands when they got themselves united and prepared to face every trouble. He then explained to the audience rules of the Jirgah and duties of its members. He said that it had been formed to free the country from foreign yoke. Those who opposed it were Government's supporters. Mr Caroe, Deputy Commissioner, Peshaw ar, had visited Tehkal and had asked people to form another Jirgah of the "Green Shirts" to counteract the activities of the Khudai Khidmatgars. This Jirgah was given an Islamic name, and was acting under the instructions of the Government. an Advocating the use of Khaddar he remarked that some people thought that Mahatama Gandhi, by urging the boycott of for- eign goods, wanted to benefit the Hindu Mihl owners, but it was wrong as Khaddar was made of home-spun yarn by the weavers who were mostly Muhammadan. By using Khaddar they could get themselves freed from the British yoke. The British, who had come here from a distant country, were enjoying their wealth while they themselves were dying of hunger. He appealed for the enlistment of red-shirts and asked people to keep them- selves prepared for a non-violent struggle.
There was no religious objection to the wearing of the red clothes, moreso, when there enemies got terrified at mere sight of them. These, however, should be worn at meetings only. Those who were weari wearing green clothes were acting under the instructions of Mr: Caroe.
The meeting ended with a prayer at about 7-15 p.m. After the dispersal of the meeting some persons collected abdul Ghafur around Arbab Khan of rehkal who delivered a short sex

c_red_shirt_4_banamari_incident_pdf_p011.text:
CONFIDENTIAL.
Ba
D.O. No.1638/283. 8.B.
CRIMINAL INVESTIGATION DEPARTMENT, North-West Frontier Province,
PESHAWAR, the 24. March, 1933.
My dear Hay,
Will you please refer to your letter No.1139, dated the 15th March, 1933, regarding the issue of rahdaris, as far as Chitral, to Sharif and Juma?
We have nothing on record against the former, but Juna appears to be identical with a man of that name and parentage who resides normally in Bhanamari, Peshawar. If it is actually the same man, it may interest you to know that he was formerly a Redshirt Captain and that he served three months' imprisonment under sections 341/143/114 I.P.C., in connection with the holding up of a police lorry by redshirts near Peshawar on 11th August 1931. Juma Khan of Bhanamari is at present absent from his residence and is believed to have gone towards Dargai but the reports are conflicting If anything objectionable comes to notice regard- ing his travels I shall be grateful for a report. It is just possible that he is acting as a link between
Peshawar Redshirts and undisrables outside the district.
Yours sincerely,
Capt. W.R. Hay, I.A.,
Political Agent,
Dir, Swat and Chitral,
MALAKAND.
No: 1639/2830-so
Sa. H.J. Vickers.
Copy, with a copy of letter to which it is a reply, forwarded for information, to the Intelligence Staff Officer i/e Intelligence Bureau, and Peshawar.
Enc: 1
Asstt. to Inspector-General of Police, C.I.Department, N.-W.F.P.

This morning 11.8.31) about 8.a.m.
one female aged about 40 years resident of Dheri Baghbanan has been run down from the back side by a lorry which was occupied by the District Police Guard who were proceeding on duty. The incident took place jus opposite to the shop of Dr: Sujan Singh which is situated just near the street leading to village Bhanamari. The female hage received serious injuries on her head and legs and has been admitted in the Lady Reading Hospital, Peshawa: where she is lying in precarious state of health. Soon after the incident the Red Shirts and
The
some shop keepers locality assembled around the lorry just near the spot. They did not allowed the guard to proceed further till the British officers arrived on the spot.
The Additional Superintendent Police, Assistant Superintendent Police, Captain lepper etc visited the spot after they received the information of the incident.
Haider Shah,President of the Youth League of Bhanamari took active party against the Police
Dham Ram 11.8.31

The following volunteers were arrested today for picketing the liquor shop in the Qissa Khani and Dalgaran Bazars, Peshawar City:-

2. Jamroz, son of Hastam, of Agra village, age about 26-27 years.
3. Ahmad Gul, son of Ahmad Beg, of Agra village, age about 24-25 years.
4. Shirin, son of Mir Hamza, of Agra village, age about 22-23 years.
5. Qabil Shah, son of Mehrban Shah, of Agra village, age about 23-24 years.
6. Ain-ud-Din, son of Mohi-ud-Din, of Agra village age about 18/19 years.
7. Muhammad Raza, son of Muhammad Usman, of Agra village, age about (?).

Nos:7 and 8 were distributing copies of cyclostyled Urdu poster, which runs as follows:-
"A second Notice to the cloth sellers of Peshawar City".
"The cloth-sellers of Peshawar City are again in- formed that they should immediately ineditely in- form us of their intention whether, or not, they wish to give us their written pledge already asked for? This notice is meant for both the retail and the whole-sale dealers. Will all our cloth-seller brethren think it sufficient and inform us of their intention ?, if not, they should remember that no allowance will be made for them hereafter, when re- pentence will be of no avail. Do our cloth-seller brethren wish us to become victims of the police beatings? Do they Wish us to be imprisoned and fil: the jails? Is it your wish that we should shed our blood on the ground in front of your shops and

Police Department. Confidential
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 24-4-31.
Peshawar Diatrict
ng
The following are the details of the demonstrations held in Peshawar City in connection with the "Martyrs' Day" on 23-4-31.
A procession of the Jamiat-1-Naujawanan-1-Islam, Peshawar, left its office at about 12 in the noon and after parading the main bazars stopped at the office of the Khilafat Committee, Peshawar, The number of the pro- cessionists was about 26. They were carrying & red cloth placard with the inscription "Muhammad Ali Ragiment No:2 under the Command of the Jamiat-1-Nau jawanan-1-Islam, Peshawar". They were raising shouts of 'Allah-O-Akbar'.
B
At about 2-45 p.m. a combined procession of the Khilafat and Sadat Committees, and the Jamiat-1-Nau jawa- nan-1-Islam, Peshawar, left the Khilafat office for the Congress office. They were singing Khilafat songs and shouting 'Allah-o-Akbar'.
At about 1-30 p.m. the women of Sadar Bazar arrived in the City in 4 lorries. They were lodged in the Dharam Sala of Mai Rajji where the City women had already assembled. At about 3 p.m. a procession of about 90 women left the Dharam Sala for the Congress office. The processionsists sang national songs en route.
At about 2-45 p.m. a procession of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha started from Karimpura and proceeded to the Congress office. The processionists raised revolutionary cries en route.
singly or
The members of the Akal1 Jatha took out no proces- sion but came to the Congress office XXX in small parties.
At about 2-30 p.m. a procession of small boys who called themselves members of the Bal Bharet Sabha, left
Lr
T

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p101.text:
(2)
Karimpura Bazar for the Congress office. They were raising revolutionary cries.
At about 3-20 p.m. a combined procession of all the above named societies was taken out from the Congress office. Later at about 3-35 p.m. a contingent of the red- shirts of Charsadda, who had arrived by lorries with their leaders, Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Abdul Akbar Khan, Milan Jaafar, Shah, and others, also joined the procession near the Congress office.
As the procession reached near the site of the 'Mar-. tyrs' Memorial in the Kissa Khani Bazar, Mian Jaafar Shah Pir Bakhsh, pleader, and Sajjad Hussain of Garhi Sayyidan mohalla, threw flowers at the said place. After this when the members of the Khilafat Committee, Sadat Committee, Jamiat-i-Naujawanan-i-Islam, the Akeli Jatha, had passed on towards the Kabull gate, and Roshan lall with two others of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha, was offering flowers at the site of the so-called "Martyrs' Memorial" a nois resembling that created by the falling of an empty kerosi oil tin from a flight of stairs was heard. At this somebod
cried out that firing had started. A Confusion followed. The volunteers of all the societies, except the red-shirt threw down their flags etc., and rushed towards the side- : streets to save their lives. Many of them took refuge unde platforms of the shops, and rushed towards the women who ap so joined the flight leaving behind their shoes and 'Dopattas'. Some of them are said to have received slighAnjurie
This state of things continued about 10 or 15 minutes afteri which Abdul Ghaffar Khan offered flowers at the site of thad "Martyrs' Memorial". He then passed his red-shirts, the Congress volunteers, and the women processionists into Dhakki Naalbandi. Other volunteers who had run away, on being assured that there had been no firing, came back an took up their flags. They then proceeded to the Shal Bagh

plice Department. onfidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 24-4-31.
Peshawar District.:
उ
1. On 23-4-31, at about 7 p.m. three men carrying bundles of shoes, etc., which had been collacted from the Qissa Khani Bazar after the procession had passed, were seen visiting the dtreets of the Andarshehr with the object of restoring these articles to their owners.
2. Rumours are current in the City that the C.I.D. men were responsible for the confusion in yesterday's pro- cession.
3. On 23-4-31, at about 10 a.m., about 100-125 persons collected in the Chowk of Gunj, 'C' Dvn., Peshawar City. Cooked rice was distributed to the poor and prayers offered for the 'Martyrs'. Ghulam Samdani, tailor of Asiya, read out the poem which had recited in the Shahi Bagh meeting the same evening. Mulla Khanmir 'Hilali' of Peshawar delivered a speech saying that the people had shown cowardice in Qissa Khani Bazar that evening. Nothing could be obtained without offering sacrifices. They would very shortly be called upon to offer sacrifices in the cause of Freedom. He concluded with the remark that if the people were not prepared for this, he would give up their society.
The meeting ended at 11 p.m.
4. Copies of a poster headed the 'Statement' and bearing the signatures of Bhagat Singh and Dat were distributed in Peshawar City by the members of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha on 23-4-31. This poster was published long ago and contains/refutation of the allegation of Police etc.,

against the two signatories.

1. On 5-5-31, at about i p.m., a Sikh was noticed bringing out some pieces of cloth from the Katra of Jowala Sahai, Andar Shehr, Peshawar City. As he came out Madat Khan, thei Congress volunteer onduty, stopped him and questioned him regarding the cloth he was carrying. The volunteer then took him to Allah Bakhsh 'Barqi', Jt. Secretary of the Congress Committee, who was sitting at the shop of a cloth merchant in the Ram Chauk Bazar, Allah Bakhsh 'Barqi' gave the volunteers some instructions and the Sikh was taken back to the Katra where he stored the cloth to the owner. Incidents of similar nature continued to happen throughout the day.
2. The picketing of the cloth shops in the Andarshehr and Ram Chauk Bazars, was resumed by the Congress volunteers at 7 p.m. today.wa
3. On 5-5-31, at about 3-30 p.m. a small National Flag was hoisted on the shop of Sant Ram, cloth merchant, Ram Chauk Bazar, to signify that he had satisfied the Congress Committee that there was no foreign cloth in his shop, and that he was allowed to resume business.

The Government had herself acted against the Law and -ed Order of which she boasts so touch. The Police officers were asked to show the warrant for the arrest of the Maulvi but they replied that they themselves were the 'Orders'. Such events caused a bad affect on the Gandhi-Irwin truce. Maulvi Ahmad Dinghad been arrested for committing no offence Abdul Rab Nashtar', pleader, delivered a speach saying that Maulvi Ahmad Din had been arrested innocently. The Government should, respecting the Gandhi-Irwin truce, createlts confidence in the minds of the public. If in her opinion the Maulvi was to deliver a bad speech, she should have taken action in accordance with the proper law. The Folice officers who had effected the Maulvi's arrest did not be- have well. The Government should hold an inquiry into this matter. The Government could have easily got rid of the Maulvi by expelling him under the Frontier Crimes Regulation as it had done in other similar cases. The Government, by avoiding to take action under the F.C.R. in this case, had exposed her weekness in that, if she had done so, it would have added another cuse to the material which would be collected by the Committee for the Investigation of the Repressive Laws. By arresting Maulvi Ahmad Din Government had disgraced the Peshawaris and the N.J.B.S of whom he was a guest.

1. On 30-5-31, at about 4 p.m. a party of 7 or 8 khilafe volunteers proclaimed in the City that a procession would be taken out at 4 p.m. on 31-5-31 and a meeting held at 9 p.m. the same evening in memory of the "Maryrs' of 31-5-31.

He then made a speech saying that the people should take a lesson from the incidents like those of 23-4-30 and 31-5-30, The Government was exercising oppression on inno- cent people. The innocent children of Sardar Ganga Singh were killed. It showed that Government would destroy them all, young and old, if they ever demanded their rights. They Government had practiced tyrannies on the innocent people of India. It had shot down people at Cawnpore and in the Jallianwala Bagh, Amritsar, and had given a proof of its unworthiness by sheding their blood in the Qissa Khani Bazar on 23-4-31 ages. It cherished so much hatred against them that it fired at the funeral proces- made sion of Serdar Ganga Singh's children. He b% an appeal for unity and said that death was better than slavery. If all of them united, there would neither remain oppression nor the oppressor. Addressing the had been Muslims he said that they a ruling nation for about 750 years, but now they were slaves. They should come to their senses. If they could not help in the progress of their country, they should at least, have sympathy with it. o They were very low in the eyes of the world. They should come forward and work under the Congress. They should

show to the Government that the Indians, whether Muslims or Hindus, were one and the same. If they united, there would be a united rule. They must be free. If they did not unite and got themselves freed, then the blood of the 'Martyrs' would prove to be of no consequence. Pir Bakhsh, pleader, Secretary, Provincial Congress Committee, delivered a speech saying that he had been in the jail for one year for serving the 'Martyrs' of 23-4-31. The Martyrs' Memorial' was demolished on the 19tion of May 1930. The second shooting incident occurred on 31-5-30. At the time of the occurrence of the latter two incidents he had been detained in the jail. The blood of the 'Martyrs' had not proved fuitless. The Hon'ble the Chief Commissioner had admitted that the Frontier people were not only civilised but were also entitled to the same rights as the rest of India. They were not lagging behind any of the nations of the world. Addressing the audience he said that they deserved congratulations as they had sacrificed the lives of their children. He would, however, say that the Government had not made a proper use of violence. "Where were Government's bullets", Ings he remarked, "when General Townshend had surrendered to thent Turks with 4000 men". He also reminded Government of what had happened at Gallipoli and Adrinople, and said that the Government had, in attacking unarmed people in Baombay and Karachi, given a proof of its hatred and con- tempt against the Indians. These incidents had left a black spot on the skirt of the Government. He supported the resolution read out by Rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi' and concluded with an appeal for the use of country made cloth and boycott of foreign goods.
After his speech the resolution mentioned above was passed unanimously, and the President put forward the

The President got up again and said that incidents like that about which they had just passed a resolution would continue to happen. If they wished to save their inno.18 cent children and their women they must attain freedom. He further said that they must compare their present condition'. with the past. They were the poorest people in the world. The income of an American was about Rs.9/- a day while that of an Indian was not more than -/1/3 or -/1/6 per day. The people of the other countries of the world hated them so much that an Indian could not enter a hotel st in Europe, etc. The reason of this was that they were slaves. Theywere called "Black men" and were being turned F. out from other countries. He appealed to the audience to help the Congress, and instead of holding separate meetings to hold combined ones. He also appealed for the enlistment of volunteers for the Congress. He said that the sale of foreign cloth had been stopped in Bannu but was sorry to see that in Peshawar, people had not given up the use of foreign made cloth. He appealed for the boycott of the foreign goods.

Referring to the firing incidents of Peshawar he said that a slave nation could not get freedom as long as such things did not happen. This meeting of the Peshawar public announces that it has no faith in the Frontier Reforms Committee, and ve appeals to the nation to boycott it. The meeting also disapproves the proposals made by the said Committee in regard to the Frontier Province".

Internal Section Diary dated 4-6-31.
On the evening of the 2nd June, 1931, Chan Shen, on of Mir Zaman Shah, of Gerhi Mir Ahmad shah, convened a meeting in Mohallah Moti, (B Divn:) in which about 30 red shirts of Tahkal Bala and about 40 red shirts of Kotla Mohsan Khan also participated. In the meeting Abdul Ghafur Khan and Hazrat Shah of Tahkal Bala and Mian Ahmed Shah, Bar-at-Law delivered speeches, while Chan Shah in the Chair. Man Ahmad Shah spoke on the duties of red shirts who were pledged to be non-violent. He further stated that the N.-W.F.P. is not inferior in any respect of any other province of India and consequently the same rights and privileges should be conceded to us as are enjoyed by other provinces. The present truce is a short lived one and if our demands ar not accepted there is likely to be restart of non-violen
18-26
fight. Red shirts should be enlisted in large number Order of our Central body should be awaited. Use Khadar, buy country made articles, control your expenses and
dispense with customary ceremonies.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan spoke on unity, exhorting the audience to give up all their differences.
He moved the following two resolutions which were unanimously carried out:-
1. This meeting has no confidence in the Frontier Reforms Enquiry Committee and urges on the Frontier public to disassociate themselves from the Committee.

A party of 7 or 8 women of Andarshehr, under the leadership of Saraswati and Goma Devi mother of Nanak Chand, pleader, Peshawar City, started from Andar Shehr carrying a National Flag, at about 5 p.m. on 10-6-31. They were singing National songs and were raising the cries of Long Live Revolution", etc. On reaching karimpura they were joined by Nikko Devi and 10 or 12 other women, and about 25-30 Congress volunteers wearing tri-colour sashes under the command of Muhammad Usman and Haji Karam Ilahi. The procession marched on to Chakka Gali where Nikko Devi asked the Sikh women to join the demonstration but they refused to do so as the National Flag did not contain the yellow stripe representing their community. They pro- made a siapa before the houses of Jaswant cessionists mad Singh and Gopal Dass of Chakka Gali and Rampura respective ly, both of whom are dealers in foreign cloth. After march ing through the streets of Karimpura, Chowk Yadgar, and the streets of Andarshehr, the procession terminated at the Arya Samaj, Dhakki Naalbandi, at about 7-30 p.m. The Congress volunteers marched in a procession to their office.

A meeting of the District Congress Committee, Peshawarment was held in the Shahi Bagh at about 7 p.m. on 21-6-31, un-it th der the presidency of Agha Casim Jan. The audience number-1- ed about 600-700.
Ghulam Samdani, tailor, read out one of his poems on the Frontier Regulations.
>I
. Act
ads.
Khan Mir 'Hilali', President of the City Congress Com-ions mittee, read out one of his 'Pushto' poems on Freedom. iee Hakim Abdul Jalil, local suspect, made a speech con- 1-30. demning the Frontier regulations which, he remraked, lad were being taken advantage of by the Government officials nt in exercising repression on the people. Vioces had been hich raised against these regulations on various occasions but the Government, which was of course not a representative Government and kept always its own motives in view, paid her no heed to them. She never cared for its subjects whose condition was getting worse day by day. Explaining the ob- ject of the present meeting he remarked that the Govern- ment had now felt the necessity of appointing a Committee to inquire and report as to what extent the people were Justified in disliking these regulations, and that if theise were repealed, what would be the result. They should how- ed the object one of ever know that the Commissions appointed by the Government had never been to relieve people from their troubles. Their real object was to while away time as long as the re agitation did not subside. It was well known to all that when the Frontier people had demanded that Reforms which had been granted to other Provinces in India should also be extended to this Province, the Government had appointed a Committee to inquire and report into the matter. This
98-

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p147.text:
(2)
Committee made enquiries, recorded statements, and sub- mitted a report to the Government with its recommendations This report, however, remained shelved with the Government for about 6 years, and it was with some difficulty that th the authorities condescended to publish it for the in- formation of the public. The officials of the Frontier Government, who were invested with the powers of a "Pharoah" of which the effect was not less than that of machine guns, however, did not allow these recommendations to be carried into effect. Again, the Suleiman Committee was appointed to report on the firing incident of 23-4-30. Statements of some persons were recorded but nothing had yet been published for their information. The Government 1s said to have accepted one of the recommendations which is yet under the veil of secrecy. Habib Nur had been hanged after the trial of a single day under the Ghazi Act His execution made the people realize that in the said Act there was a sword hanging constantly over their heads. They started an agitation against it. The Government appointed a Committee to enquire and report if this and the other oppressive laws which were in force in the Frontier Province should be repealed or not. But the Committee would not prove of any consequence like those which had been appointed previously. The course adopted by it indicates that it would do nothing for them. The people of the Frontier wwre not behind others in any res- pect. Their moral, financial, and social conditions were better than the others. He was therefore at a loss to understand why they had been subjected to the oppressive laws which were not in force in the rest of India. He the proposed the following resolution:-
"This meeting of the Peshawar public announces that it has no confidence in the Inquiry Committee and the course adopted by it and demands immediate re- peal of the repressive laws which were in force in the Frontier Province. It also considers that the
are

Pundit Amir Chand, Barwal, and Dr: C.C. Ghosh support- ed the above resolution.

1. The cloth merchents of Peshawar City kept their shops closed on 14-1-31, for fear of the picketers.
At 12 o'clock on 14-1-31, they held & maging in the Dharamsala of Mai Rajji, at which it was decided that the foreign cloth lying in their shops should be disposed of and its further purchase should be stopped. All of them, with the exception of a few, signed an undertaking to the above effect. This undertaking will be handed over to the Picketing Committee, Charsadda,

Continuing, he said that 150 years before the Indians 1 could make the British dance like monkeys but the latter were now teaching them civilisation. India had produced brave kings like Akbar, Aurangzeb, Rana Partab, Ranjit Sing.." etc. The British thought that the Indians were not fit to rule their own country but boys from the London Colleges who did not know how to wash their faces, were. The British y had carried away the wealth of India to England, Carun (Croesus) used to exhume dead bodies to take of their coffins but the British had made money by pounding down their bones. The Indians well knew that the British had -it reduced them to powerty. It now remained to be seen whether the Indians, who had collected under the Congress flag, were fit to attain liberty for their country or not. The You young men had done a work under the Congress had given

flag that had no example. They gave their lives for the country on 23-4-30. It was not an easy thing to become a "Martyr" In this world people were killing each other for selfish motives but to become a "Martyr" in the cause of freedom was a different thing. He had, during his tour, studied the people of the Frontier and had found them true men, for which he congratulated them. All the men and women of India were now prepared to struggle for the freedom of their country. Five years back it was not without some risk that one went out of Peshawar City. The other day when he alighted at the City Railway station he asked the driver to take him to the area where people used to be looted. He took him about 15 miles on the other side of the f river but no one came to loot them. The driver told him

if they wore not protected by them. Mahatma Gandhi had told the people they should become united if they wanted to attain Swaraj. He accused Government of croating communal disturbances and wanted the people to know it. He further said that when they had promoted unity amongst themselves no power of the world would be able to separate them, and asked people to prepare for Liberty although the path leading to it was strewn with thorns. The flower of Liberty" was just like a rose and when they had passed over the thorns they would smell the gent of the rose. They should remember that the Government would try all sorts of tricks to put them against each other. As a resident of Bombay he was telling them what was plain truth. They want- ed British Imperliasm to be rooted out from its foundations, They wanted to bury it in so deep a ditch that it might not spring up again. They were always prepared to crush it.
He continued his speech with the remarks that if Bhagat Singh, Raj Guru, Sukh Devi, etc., had been hanged and some of them (the Indians) had been shot down it was an ordinary thing % if some of the dirty germs (he referred to himself and the audience) would be killed for serving India. They should offer sacrifices for the freedom of their country for which they would have to un- dergo troubles. The object of Abdul Ghaffar Khan in wander- ing in the streets was to fill the bellies of the poor and not to obtain wealth for himself. He was already sufficient- ly rich. Mahatama Gandhi's object in wandering in the country in a naked state was to serve the agriculturists and not to collect wealth for himself. He wanted the poor and the agriculturists, who had been ruined through oppres- sion to get up and establish their Kingdom in India.

Replying himself he said that it was Congress had only. Bome years ago the Congress/hoisted its flag under had now which thousands of people/collected. The women should also come forward and take part in the movement. Hundreds of the and brethren had been hanged exiled for struggling for freedom. They had been shot down at Jallianwalla Bagh and in Peshawar. Their sisters had been beaten with lathis and dragged by hair by the "ritish. If they

to know that the Hindus and Sikhs of Peshawar were not taking an adequate part in the movement for Liberty. Addressing the Hindus and Sikhs he said that if they wanted to live they should join the Congress. They should % know that the Muslims of the Frontier had decided to take no rest as long as they had not attained Liberty. (9)
Kohat who made a speech in which he said that they should ཀྭ་་འ་ offer sacrificos to gourd their honour. Referring to the remarks of the Hon'ble the Chief Commissioner that the Frontier people deserved equal rights with the rest of India, he said that a Reforms Inquiry Committee had been appointed to report on the question of the extension of Reforms to this Province, and another Committee was making investigations in connection with the Frontier Regulations. The people of the Frontier, had, by offering sacrifices, proved that they would not obey the repressive laws which were in force in this Province. The real object of the Government, in deputing the above two committees, was to show to the world that the Frontier people did not deserve what they desired. The course that had been adopted by the Reforms Committee was wrong. The Frontier people were subjected to unspeakeable repressions. The case of Bhagat Singh etc. had been tried for years while that of Habib Nur had been decided after a trial of 24 hours. The laws referred to had been framed by the British who had come hera from a country thousands of miles away. H.E. the Viceroy wanted to enforce these laws on them as these had been made by white people. The reeponsiblity for the back- Lan wardness of the Frontier people in education lies on Government itself. The Congress Committee was doing a work for them which the subject Committee could not do. It was shameful for such a big Government to enforce such laws as were in force here. By enforcing such laws the Government had prooved that it was God's agent (Khalifa-tul-lah) and was killing human being like a butcher killed goats. In spite of their stupidity the British thought they were wise. If they were men they should treat others like human beings,

In the mean time about 50 Rol Shirts of Albela and Kankola while returning frou Tahkil Bala reached to the office in Chirva boban andersing the City via Katcheri Gate Khan Abdul Ghaffar dan delivered a short #ech to the Hand Cart Drivers saying to be strict upon the principles . c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p162.text:
Internal Section Diary dated 27-6-31.
On the evening of 26-6-31 at about 4-30 p.m. a procession of the red shirts (hand cart drivers), about 240 in number, included the cart drivers of Pipal Mandi, Lakkar Mandi, Railway Station and red shirts of Bhanamari started from Chauk Yadgar having their flag and drum. After passing through the main bazaars of the City they went to Shahi Bagh.
Another procession of the red shirts of Taru Jabba, Pir Piyai, Pabbi and of Sadar Police Station entering the City vie Hashtnagri Gate and after passing through the main bazaars of the city went to Shahi Bagh. Their number was about 250.They had flag of their own and drum too. The processionists raised usual revolutionary cries on their way. On reaching the Shahi Bagh the processionists joined in the meeting of the Khilafat- ists which was already in progress there. After the Khilafat meeting was over Mulla Karvi was voted to the Chair and a reso- lution demanding the Govt: to repeal repressive laws was passed Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai delivered a speech that no good will come out of these resolutions. They rendered gre help to the Govt; during the Great War and destroyed Musl kingdoms, i.e., Mecca Baghdad, etc., for their sake but ey this failed to win their friendship. They are not going to pleased easily by the Indians. Thanks heaven that the Indiens have comt to realise the English people in good time. The Indians will not be enteruped by them. It is pity that the English enjoy their luxurious meals five times a day which on the other hand a majority of the Indians go about without meal! They look down upon the Indians as inferiors and send them to jails. They will try to create a split among the Indians by spending their money. It is evident that the English are res- ponsible for all what is being done now a days. The Indians are suffering for the cause of their country. It is their reque to those Hind us, Muslims and Sikhs who help the Govt; for
pleasure that they get in this manner, is temporary one. a time for them to learn a lesson to help the cause of th country. The English would never yeild by entreaties and prayers. This is the Frontier Province where oppressive are enforced. The people of Prontier patiently endured

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p163.text:
shots which were fired upon them and the result was that they
won their respect of the country. The British Govt too have a
underston their worth and now ask them to put forward their demands. This is the outcome of strength. They cannot tolerat bondage any more. They are openly watching the present truce, otherwise their non-violent force is far superior to field guns, war ships and aeroplanes. They are brave people and are still observing the terms of the truce. It is only the coward that violate the terms of the truce. He then referred to the hurried execution of Habib Nur. He asked the public to unite together to fill up the jails to attain liberty. He further requested the public not to obey the oppressive laws. The Govt: will surrender when they will all turn red shirts. These people draw princely salaries by simply signing the papers while all work of administration is being done by the Indians who get a few rupees per mensem. He gave an example of Tahkal and said it is a good thing that now even the ladies have come out in the field. By travelling in down country he has come to know that great respect those people have for the Afghans. Dedicate your lives in the name of God and do your duties. The British try to provoke you. Don't be mislead by them. He exhorted the red shirts not to boycott the public but try to won them all to their side. This will result in good for the whole country.
The audience were about 2000 in number. The meeting came to close with the recitation of a national song.
(7

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p164.text:
On the afternoon of 26.6.31,a meeting of the Rod Shirts (P.S. E' division),
was held at Nauthe,under the presidency of Imam Shah
a field outside the village leuthe. About 2000 people attended including 1000 Red Shirts and 500 females from Nauthe,Bhanamari, Charkhana,Sufed Dheri, Kotla and Landi.
a
The proceedings were started with the recitation of national song on liberty which was recited by 2 boys. Nasrulla Khan of Charkhana delivered a speech in which he said that some days ago a Hindu came to the President of the Red Shirts of Sarband asking him to render him assistance in getting back his debt. As a result he presi-- dent sent for the debtor and managed for the return of the money. When the lamberdars and Khans of the village heard about it they made a secret report to the Deputy Commissione with the result that the President was arrested without any fault. The Deputy Supdt Police had been telling to the debtor that he should make a statement that the amount he paid, was taken from him forcibly but he refused to do so. The speaker remarked that in this way thether the Govt is making justice. Neither there is any complaint against the President in the Police nor in the court. They are not afraid of the jails. The following resolutions wese moved and unanimously passed: -
1. That this meeting of the Red Shirts of IllaqaKhalil and Mohmands strongly demands of the Govt to release their men arrested from, Tehkal and Sarband.
half of
2. That the Revenue should be remitted be taken in the form of a
glain.
and the rest should
3. The Tonga, house and " Hasiat" taxes should be remitted as the public is not in favour of its payment.
He Nasrulla Khan) protested against the arrest
of Abdul Ghaffur Khan of Tehkal Baland said that his
Baland
arrest was www due to the complaint of the Khans etc. He urged the Government to release him. Hefurther emphasised upon the audience that unless they would not come out in the feild they cannot be successful. Then they have got nothing to feed themselves how they could pay the Revenue

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p165.text:
and hence they were compelled to fight for it. He would not say this that the Revenue should not be paid but D those he could not afford,should avoid of its payment, untill the revenue is remitted. He related a story
regarding the cheapness of the vegetables, that one day in these days a vegetable seller brought a Tonga of the vegetable in the City to sell, but he could not make up the hire of the Tonga. Under these cicumstances how the Zamindars could pay the revenue. Upon which the audience raised a united voice that the Revenue should not be paid. He further stated that Abdul Ghafur Khan and one Maulvi of Landi 'have been dismissed from their services on account of participating in a meeting recently held at Thkel Bala.
One Maulvi of Nauthe aged about 50 years also made a speech. He appealed to the audience to create unity among them and asked them to follow their principles strictly even they are sent in lock up.
The meeting came to close after the recitation of few versus from " Qoran". The following were prominent in the meeting:-
1. Sayid Imam Shah.
2, Sayid Usman Shah. 3, Nasrullah Khan.
4. Azimillah Khan
5. Imam Masjid of Nauthe. 6. Mulla Sahib Landi.
Dhann Ram 27.6131

CONFIDENTIAL DIARY OF A.S.P. CITY FOR 18th JANI 1931.
6 mon were arrested in "A" Division for picketting yesterday. All are residents of Jalabela Police Station Daudrai.
8 men were arrested in "B" Division yesterday for pioketting. All are residents of Charsadda. The mon arrested in A" Division were speedod on their way with a few muffled revolutionary ories but a Polico chargo was not necessary. There was no demonstration at all in "B" Division.
2 potassium crackers wore thrown yesterday after-noon without doing any damage. Both wore obviously manufactured by the same man who is as yet untraced.

Peshawar City, was fined Rs. 101/- for purchasing two bles of foreign cloth in contravention of the undertaking

cently. The money has been handed over Committee.

A cyclostyled 'Urdu' poster, headed Teat of the Musalmans of Peshawar', and asking Musalmans to refrain from wearing foreign clothes on the occasion of the coming 'Id', and from taking part in fairs etc., was distributed in Peshawar City on 14.2.31. It also request the 'Ulema' to pray for the victory of the Afridis after each and every prayer.
A copy of the poster is herewith attached (for C.I.D. only).
The 'Moti Lal Nehru Day' was observed in Peshawar City by holding a partial hartal on 15.2.31. The following persons were seen asking people to close their shops: -
1) Gurbachan Singh, shopkeeper of Katha Khalsa Brothers, Peshawar City.

Ram Singh, an employee of Bhan Singh, cloth merchant of Peshawar City.

He also prayed to God for the, restoration of the peace, and said that he was very much c feeling the separation of his son. In conclusion he told. people that holding fairs and animal fights were prohibitor in Islam.
In the Idgah mosque, where the congregation numbered about 1000-1200, Maulvi Abdul Ghaffur of Machchi atta, 'C' Dvn. Peshawar City, delivered a speech in which he said that their brethren who had been killed on 23-4-30 were 'Martyrs', that flowers should be offered at their graves and their children and widows should be helped.
After this Dr: Gilani said something privately to the maulvi, on which he remarked that the poster which the Khilafat Committee was to issue in connection with the forthcoming elections, had been forfeited by the Governmen and that the Peshawar public wanted an election system sin lar to the one in force in the Punjab. Special Diary, D/- 13-3-31.
Peshawar District.
ELLIGENCE BRA
14 MAR 1938
1931
Police Department..
Confidential
Acting on the rumour that Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Agha Lal Badshah, Mian Ahmad Shah, and other recently released political prisoners were to arrive here by the AEZEL 9-45 p.m. train, some of the local Khilafat volunteers went to Cantt: Ry: stn. on 10-3-31, but returned dis- appointed as none of the leaders had arrived.
un the morning of 11-3-31, the Khilafat volunteers were noticed proclaiming in the City that the leaders had halted at Nowshera and that they would arrive here at 10 a.m. by road. The public should go to receive them outside the Hashtnagari gate. Accordingly a crowd of about 200-300 men from Charsadda and the City collected E near the Saddar police station to receive the leaders, but on a report from Nowshera that Abdul Ghaffar khan, had, after his release from the Gujrat jail, gone to Lahore, and Mian Ahmad Shah to Charsadda via Mardan, the crowd dispersed.
Nikko Devi, the well known woman agitator, and her son Roshan Lal, left for Nowshera in a motor car to see lent
the leaders. The motor car was borrowed to them by Chela Ram, Vakil, of Peshawar, and a National Flag was hanging from its side.
The other prominent persons who are reported to have gone to Nowshera to see the leaders, are Abdul Rab 'Nashtar', and Muhammad Yunas, pleaders.
In the meantime preparations were being made in the city to accord the leaders a fitting reception. The shopkeepers of Qissa Khani Bazar, Misgaran Bazar, Bazar Kalan, and Bazazan Bazar decorated their shops profusely National flags were hoisted on some of them.
C
E

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p045.text:
(2)
At about 2 p.m., Mian Ahmad Shah, Abdul Akbar, and some others arrived here from Nowshera by motor car. -- Nikko Devi and Roshan Lal were also with them. They en- tered the City through the Hashtnagari gate where a pro- cession was formed, which, after marching through Karim- pura, Chauk Yadgar, Bazazan, Cissa Khani, and Namak Man- di Bazars, terminated at the dispensary of Dr: Khan Sa- hib in Chirwa Koban, where Mian Ahmad Shah thanked the public of Peshawar for according him so good a reception and expressed his inability to deliver a lecture owing to his bad state of health.
The processionists en route raised revolutionary cries At about 6 p.m., Pir Bakhsh, pleader, and others who had been released from Bannu jail, also arrived. They entered the City through Kabuli gate and after parading through the main bazars in a procession marched on to the dispensary of Dr: Khan Sahib, where the procession ter- minated. The procession stopped near the Khilafat office where Pir Bakhsh delivered a short speech the purport of which was that he (Pir Bakhsh) was an ordinary man and had not yet served the country in the manner he wished. The processionists en route raised shouts of "Allah-0 Akbar", "Pir Bakhsh Zinda
in Zinda Bad", etc.
On 12-3-31, the City was decorated on a higher scale.

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p046.text:
(3)
Two of the triumphal arches bore the following in- scriptions:-
1. "Garhwali Gate, Ram Chauk, Peshawar City, by the shopkeepers of Ram Chauk Bazar
2 *Muhammad Ali Gate (Qissa Khani Bazar). Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Lal Badshah, accompanied by Mian Jaafar Shah and Ali Gul Khan, arrived here by the Frontiet Mail. A crowd of about 400-500 men from Char- sadda and Peshawar City was present to receive them at the Cantt. Ry. Stn. The leaders were sat in motor cars and driven to the Hashtnagari gate in a procession which gw thicker as it approached the City. The pro- cessionists en route shouted "Long Live Revolution", "Zalim Government Barbad", "Up Up Hindustan", "Down Down: Inglistan", etc. The procession entered the City from the Hashtnagari gate. On its arrival in the Karimpura bazar, Milap Singh delivered a short speech saying that Abdul Ghaffar Khan was the 'Mahatama Gandhi' of the Frontier Province and all welcomed him on his release from jail. The procession then passed through Pul-i-Pukh- ta, Chauk ik Yadgar, Bazazan Bazar and Qissa Khani Bazar, had been stopping near the spot where a monument way raised to the memory of the 'martyrs' of 23-4-30.
Ali Gul Khan, addressing the crowd remarked that that was the place where the 'martyrs' had shed their blood for Freedom, and it was due to their sacrifices that had
they come out successful.
Pir Bakhsh remarked that he had proposed a resolu- in a Congress meeting that the Qissa Khani Bazar should be re-named 'Shahidi Bazar'. He was very pleased to see
и
that monument had bee raised to the memory of the 'martyr but was sorry that it had been demolished with a view
to obliterate the memory of the 'martyrs' from the minds of the people. He then offered garlands of flowers on the place where the monument stood saying that the memory of

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p047.text:
(4)
the martyrs' could never be erased.
Lal Badshah said that the memory of the martyrs' would be re-built, and if that was not allowed, the other memorials would also not exist.
Abdul Rashid, son of Ghulam Hussain of Muqarrab Khan mohalla, recited some verses.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan said that that was the spot where grief and not joy should be expressed, and asked the people not to be pleased over the agreement which had been reached between them and the Government. They should try to promote unity among themselves. It was possible that they would be called upon to offer more sacrifices than they have s so far done. All of them should, there- fore, keep themselves ready for offering more sacrifices Mian Ahmad Shah said that that was the time for the unity of the Hindus, Muslims and the Sikhs.
Abdul Akbar Khan of Charsadda s said that it was the blood of the martyrs' that had attracted him to the City. He recited a 'Pushto' poem on unity. He asked people to pray for him that he may share the fate of the martyrs'.
After this prayers were offered for the martyrs and the procession marched out of the Kabuli gate and re-entered the City by the Dabgari gate. While the pro- cession was passing through the Dabgari Bazar, one Sam- dani, a tailor, read out an 'Urdu' poem congratulating the leaders on their release. He also said that they had come out successful and the face of the Government had been blackened.
Another tailor, named Ghulam Samdani, threw some copper coins over the car occupied by the leaders.
When the procession reached in Misgaran Bazar, Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Mian Ahmad Shah, Abdul Akbar, Mian Jaafar Shah, Mian Abdulla Shah and Dr: Khan Sahib, thighta feon the rece

left the prossession and drove away for Charsadda through the Kabuli gate. On arrival near the Mundi Beri Lal Badshah related to the crowd the stories of the alleged tyrannies of the authorities over the prisoners in jail. He also said that Freedom could not be attained without offering sacrifices.
praised
Fy Bakhsh supported Lal Badshah's speech and /u- A the services he had renedered to the country.
The procession terminated near the Khilafat office
at about 1-30 p.m.

the leaders and the Government, during which the question of Government's peace with the Afridis was also referred to. The discussion was, however, postponed to some future date.

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p055.text:
Police Department..
Confidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 21-3-31.
Poshayar District 47
At about 3 p.m. on 20-3-31, a procession of about 20-25 Congress volunteers, wearing tri-colour sashes, and carrying a National Flag, left the Congress office, and marching through the main bazars of the City, went on to the Shahi Bagh, where they reached at about 4-30 p.m. They en route song National songs and raised revolutionary cries. On their way they were joined by about 40-50 men from the City.
This was followed by a meeting in the Shahi Bagh. The audience at this meeting numbered about 1000-1200. Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Mian Abdulla Shah of Charsadda, Ali Gul Khan, Pir Bakhsh, pleaders, Qasim Jan, Maulvi Abdul Rahim, Haji Karam Ilahi, Fazl-i-Mahmud, Muhammad Usman, Ghulam Rabbani Sethi, Kahan Singh, Uttam Chand, and Hakim Abdul Jalil, were more prominent among the té Congressmen present.
At the proposal of Mulla Khanmir, Ali Gul occupied the chair. He thanked the audience for this honour.
Pir Bakhsh a water carrier, and Fazl-i-Mahmud of Peshawar City, recited a poem on the National Flag on which cries of Long Live Revolution", "Up Up the National Flag," "Down Down the Union Jack", etc., were raised.
Mulla Khan Mir, "Hilali", of Peshawar City, got up and said that a truce had been made between Mahatama Gandhi and Lord Irwin. He would not, therefore, say much. He recited a 'Pushto' poem the purport of which was that a nation which was devoid of education and art remains always in slavery. Only those young persons
who did not fear trouble, could attain Freedom. The tim for pleasure would be that when Freedom had been ob

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p057.text:
(3)
nd
meant. They were ready to make peace if the Government was also ready to do this. He asked the audience not to be proud (over the truce) but be calm and careful as their object was not merely to secure the release of the politi- cal prisoners, but is me to secure their rights from the Goverment. He said that bhagat 1 Fun, brother of Hari had been Kishan of Mardan, Haji Muhammad Aain of sarband who w o in spite of being a political offender, dealt with as a criminal, and others who had been arrested at Lahore, have had not yet been released. How then there could be a peace. They were acting on the instructions of Mahatama Gandhi, but the Frontier Government should also act accord- ing to those of Lord Irwin. He told the people that they should not think that they had attined Independence. They should know that only the day release of the prisoners had been secured so far. They wanted their rights and would not accept anything less than was given to the other Provinces of India. They wished much rights as would bene- fit the poor class. The Khans etc., were already satisfied by with their position. They wanted those rights as which all the Nation could be benefitted because rich classes were already satisfied. If this could not be done then he would be the first persons to re-start a non-violent struggle for the Indpendence. He advised the people not to re- think of be lazy but to a ces their children who we had nothing to wear. He appealed for the enlist of volunteers

(5)
had said that there was no need of bring the Military int the city as the people were quite ready to surrender them selves for arrest. The Sub: Inspector 'A' Division had al so informed the authorities that there was no need ng
of taking such a measure, but a group of officers who were in favour of violence to crush the movement, did what they wiched. They were fools. The country which is oppressed gets Freedom. Many people were of the opinion that the British were just, but the world had seen that there was no the other Government such barbarious and bad c as this. He said that the British soldiers entered the house of the citizens and the widows and the children were shut up in their houses and left to starve. They could not call such a Government & just one. (At this time shouts of Really, Really were raised from the audience). The Government could not rule by oppression. It had proclaimed that the people of this Province were progres- sing but it was not ready to give them anything, as the Bame officers are still in the Province and had not been transferred. Their intentions towards the people were not good. If the Chief Commissioner wishes to give them something, he should give them the same rights of Munici- pal and District elections as were in force in other Provinces in India. The prisoners DL from the Agencies had not been released so far. They had every connection with the Agencies, the Afridis, and Afghanistan. They would act upon the injunctions of Mahatama Gandhi, but the Government would not give up repression as the group officers who were in favour of repression was still present in the Province. Ali Khan delivered a speech in which he said that they were not satisfied with this truco until the hands of the Britsh were in their pockets. They had postponed the struggle for Freedom under the orders of Mahatma Gandhi, He said that it there could be no effect of this truce untill if/could not effect the Afridis, and the Garhwall prisoners. (At this cries of Long Live Garhwalls were raised). He read out the resolutions mentioned above with the addition that the per- sons convicted for taking part in the struggle for freedom, from the agencies should also be released. At this one of the audience asked suggested that Abdul Rahman alias Mhani, of Peshawar City should also be released. The President Came assured him that Abdul Rahman's name also m under the catagory of the prisoners win who should be released. In conclusion he expressed his gratitude towards the Khudai Khidmatgars, the Congress volunteers, and persons wounded, and persons who had shown sympathy with the Congress move- ment. He said that if need arose to re-start the campaign they would again be called upon to help as before. There were many of the political prisoners who were still in the Jails and if they were not released the truce may break. The meeting concluded at about 6 p.m.
The leaders and some of the people after the conclusion of the meeting prayed for the freedom of muslims and ful- filment of Abdul Ghaffar Khan's wishes, and destruction of the Government.

1. A partial 'llartal' was observed in the Badar Bazar, Peshawar Cantt: on 24-3-31.
е
un 24-3-31, at about 2 p.m., Mat. Ato, and Mst. Nihali, 10' of the Ladies Association, Peshawar Cantt:, led a procession- of about 300-400 women who had collected in the Kalabari temple, sadar Bazar. They were carrying a black flag and photos of Bhagat Singh and Jowahir Lal Nehru, and were Joined by about 300-350 men from the Peshawar Cantts. After marching through the Sadar Bazar they started for the
City singing national songs and raising revolutionary cries They reached Kabuli gate at about 5 p.m. Mohan Lal, son of n Mst. Ato was shouting that from a single drop of Bhagat XL- Singh's blood, thousands of Bhagat Singhs and Datts would crop up. un reaching Kissa Khani Bazar he said that that was the place where their brethren had given their lives for the sake of the country. The processionists on reachin Karimpuru left the ladies in Bhai Biba Singh's Gurdwara, + went on to
We cothe Shahi Bagh, to attend the meeting which was to have been held there under the presidency of Agha Lal Budshah.
2. On 24-3-31, at about 6-45 p.m. Bome 40 or 50 men and boys, while returning from Shahi Bagh stopped before the liquor shop in Dalgaran Bazar and began to throw bricks and stones on the shop. The liquor contractor closed the doors but the frezied mob broke them do and entering the shop by force began to destroy furniture, wine-bottles, etc. They entered the store room and breaking the cash box carried away its contents. The liquor contractor and his servants ran away leaving the shop at the mercy of the mob. The loss is estimated at about Rs.2000/-.
At the end of the scene some Congressmen appeared at
the spot

spot and deputed some congress volunteers to clear tho crowds which had collected outside the shop.

ng investigation into the case.

(2)
office joined by another procession which met it in
the Dabgari Bazar. On reaching the Congress office re- Ghulam Rabbani Sethielivered a speech saying that thatngs was the first occasion in Peshawar on which the ladies id wore to take part in a procession. The men should takelf a lesson from them, and should boycott foreign cloth. -10 The procession then moved to Karimpura where about 200 ho to 300 ladies also joined. They were singing national songs and raising revolutionary cries. The procession then returned to the Hasting's Memorial Chaowk where a he mob of about 200-300 persons had already collected. Here the ladies sat down and Milap Singh, political agitator, addressed them with a speech saying that the Government, after it had made a truce with the Congress! had hanged Bhagat Singh and his companions. He then recited a poem in praise of the 'Martyrs'. The mob, ex-- cluding the women, then left for Shahi Bagh, where a meeting was held under the presidency of Lal Badshah. The gathering here numbered about 1000-1500. The promi- nent of the Congressmen present were Ali Gul Khan, Mulla khanmir, Muhammad Yusaf, alias 'Chacha', Ghulam Rabbani Sethi, Rahim Bakhsh 'Chaznavi', Allah Bakhsh 'Darqi', Qasim Jan and Pir Shahinshah of Kohat.
Ghulam Samdani, tailor of Asiya, opened the pro- ceedings with a poem in praise of Bhagat Singh and Datt and saying that the young men who did not care for their lives could only attain freedom for their country Mullah Khan Mir 'Hilali', read out a Pushto poem saying that Bhagat Singh was the 'Sardar' of the young- men of India. The tyrant Government had hanged him and his companions. India was mourning while Churchill must have been pleased. Tyrant Irwin became deaf and paid no heed to the 'Jirgas' and the appeals for mercy. All Indians were Bhagat Singh Rajguruf and Sukhdev The
Government

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p075.text:
(3)
ro
Government had acted against the terms of the truce, and the young men would find out some other path. Hovo-, ngo lutionary cries were raised on the conclusion of this d
poem.
Ali Gul Khan read out the following resolution:-
де
This meeting of the public of Peshawar express ho es hatred against Government's action in hang- ing Bhagat Singh and his companions, and sym- pathy with the relatives of the deceased, and prays for the souls of the persons hanged:
He said that Bhagat Singh had thrown the bomb in the Assembly Hall with the object of warning the Govt. not
"he
and to kill any body. He said that he could not say more as the Congress had ordered that the 'Hartal' and the demonstrations should be entirely peaceful. He ad- vised the audience to boycott foreign cloth He ques- tioned the audience whether they would do so much as to boycott foreign cloth in memory of Bhagat Singh. The audience replied in the affirmative,
1 m
ahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi' then got up and made a speech EU regretting that he had to come on the stage on a day when his heart was bleeding and all the Indians, old or young, were in mourning, Bhagat Singh had given his life for the sake of the freedom of. India. He threw a bomb in the Assembly Hall, as his kcom line of serving India was different their's. The goal, however, was one and the same. Bhagat Singh had avowed that he would attain freedom or give his life inthis attempts. Those who wished to attain free- dom should always be ready to give their lives. The 'martyrs' of Peshawar gave their lives in the cause of the Freedom (Cries of Long Live Martyrs of Peshawar). He regretted that inspite of the fact that Bhagat Singh Rajguru and Sukhdev had given their lives, the people of Peshawar % were not wearing country made clothes and said that if they had any love for these 'Martyrs'

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p076.text:
(4)
do
'martyrs' they should some practical work and not more. ly confine their activities to processions and meetings If they had any sympathy with Bhagat Singh they should wear mourning dress as long as India was not freed. If God wished, the blood of the 'martyrs' would bring some result. Referring to the case of Habib Nur, Mohmand, ho remarked that one of the assessors was a tailor and the other two were, a contractor and carpenter. Habib Nur had been tried one day and hanged the next day. The real monument to the memory of the 'Martyrs' would be to wear Khaddar. He suggested that a committee of the cloth merchants should be formed to botcott foreign cloth, otherwise not a single person of Peshawar would remain alive if the stopping of the sale of the foreign cloth was left to them. It was both, an order and a re- quest, that all foreign goods should be boycotted. (Cries of Long Live Revolution etc.)
Ghulam Samdani, tailor of Asiya, read out an Urdu ing
doing
poem on the advantages of weary Khaddar by which they could destroy the factores of England and make the British die of hunger.
Lal Badshah said that they were working under the instructions of the Congress Committee, and had re- ceived the following telegram from the All India Con- gress Committee:-
"Bhagat Singh and party executed
condolence
and peaceful 'Hartal' should be made - no · meetings..
etc..
He said that the case of which Bhagat Singh was charged was not proved against them. The object of the thorwing of the bomb in the Assembly Hall was not to
cause injury to any human being.

1. On 26-3-31, at about 10 a.m., about 100-150 Hindu muslim and Sikh boys, marched through the streets of Sadar Bazar, Peshawar, asking shopkeepers to observe a 'Hartal'. They were carrying a National Flag and shout- ing Toady Bachcha Hai Hai", etc. They were dispersed by the Police.
2. On 26-3-31, at about 11-30 a.m. a party of 6 or 7 Congress volunteers, who were carrying a National Flag and beating a drum, announced in the City Bazars that no procession should be taken out by any irresponsible person and if any one did so the Congress would not be responsible for it.
n-
3. At about 12 O'Clock on 26-3-31, a procession of the Hindu and Sikh women of Asiya mohalla was taken out from Kalu mohalla 'B' Division, Peshawar City. The num- ber of the woman processionists was about 100-125. Abou 100-150 men also joined them. They were carrying three National flags and three black flags and were raising the cries of 'Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Bhagat img Singh", etc. and performing 'Siapa' (to recita dirge) on their way. On reaching outside Bajauri gate, Ghulam /Samdani tailor, read out two poems one on freedom and
the other on the advantages of wearing "Khaddar". The processions is raised shouts of "Boycott foreign cloth". The procession re-entered the City through Kabuli Gate. They stopped near the place where the 'Martyrs' Memoria had stood and performed the 'Siapa'. One Mst. Lali of Kalu mohalla stood on a chair reciting the dirge and raising revolutionary cries. The crowd swelled as it moved towards the Bazazan Bazar, where the procession- ists asked the cloth merchants to shut their shops.
.0

(2)
The procession marched on Chauk Yadgar, Pul-1-Pukhta and Karimpura Bazar terminating at Bhai Biba Singh's Gurdwara at about 3 p.m. A Hindu woman recited a poem in praise of the 'Martyrs'. The cloth,erchants of Bazazan, Chauk Yadgar, Pul1-1-Pukhta and Karimpura Bazars shut their shops and went on 'Hartal'
4. On 26-3-31, at 12 o'clock, a procession of about 300-350 women (Hindu and Sikh), started from Babu mohalla, Sadar Bazar. They were carrying two flags one of which was the 'National' and the other a black one. About 400-500 men of all classes also joined them. The procession after marching through the streets of Sadar Bazar marched towards the City. The processions te sang XXXXXX national songs and recited dirges on their way. Mohan Lala, son of Ato addressed the crowd saying that Bhagat Singh and his party had been hanged on the evening of 23-3-31, and their dead bodies were taken to the banks of Satluj river and burnt with kerosene oil instead of wood. He also said that us on hearing the news of the execution of Bhagat Singh, Dutt had committed suicide in jail. On reaching near the site of the "Martyrs' Memorial' he said that that was the place where the 'Martyrs' of Peshawar had given their lives in the cause of the freedom of their country. He remarked that the Government had deceived Congress in executing Bhagat Singh and his party. All should, therefore, now act on the instructions of the Congress, boycott foreign goods and wear country made clothes. The procession then marched through the main Bazars of the City and terminated at Bhai Biba Singh's Gurdwara in Karimpura Bazar at about 4-30p.m. The men dispersed and the 'Chautha' ceremony was per- formed by the women who had collected in the Gurdwara building. During the performance of the ceremony no man was allowed to enter the Gurdwara.

4.3.2. CITY OR 13/0
A brief emmary of the event of 13/931 14 attached
3 pat.
plaketters were arrested on the liquor shops In the Rasaldian! without any trouble. Shortly afterwards men with flowers round theis necks were noticed moving round the streets followed by crowds of people raising revolutionary cries. Extra strength was brought to the Kissakhand from "c" Division and the men who were shouting were
arrested.
At 3.15 p.m. information was received that similar parties were moving round in Chouk Yadgar. Extra strength was immediately sent from Gorkhatri. Before this arrived however a Hindu constable who was there in plain clothes was assaulted by the crowd for pointing out to a Sub-Inspector a man who was shouting "Inqilab". It was with difficulty that the S.I. rescued him from the crowd. A.S.P. City and Addl. strength then arrived on the spot and Chouk Yadgar was cleared. Extra strength from Cantonments, and Reserve Guard were
sent for.
Further bodies of demonstrators were now moving about in the area between "C" Division and Misgaran Chouk. The Kissakhani was cleared and pickets placed, and after this patrols were sent out as a result of which the crowd gradually dispersed and normal conditions were reached at 6 p.m. All Police were withdrawn by 7 p.m. The total number of persons arrested was 18 and of whom 3 were arrested for inciting the crowd.
The temper of the crowd appeared worse then it has been since the beginning of picketting and shouts were continually raised. The crowd however appeared to consist chiefly of loafers and small boys. Force was only used by the Police on one or two occasions.
Dated.
14/231

The procession of yesterday was followed by a meet- ing in the Shahi Bagh at 6 p.m. under the presidency of Maulvi Abdul Hakim, Pol: Agitator, Peshawar. The audience numbered about 2000-2500.
Lr
Ghulam Samdani of Asiya mohalla read a poem describir ing the events of 23-4-30 and accusing the Government of high-handedness, etc.
Mulla Khan Mir 'Hilali' read out a poem in Pushti saying that Sir Norman Bolton, Mr: Metcalfe, and Mr: Isemonger were resposible for the incident of 23-4-30, and praying God to take their revenge from the Government Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai put forward the
following resolution:-
This meeting of the public of Peshawar prays for the pure souls of the Martyrs' of Peshawar, who had sacrificed their lives for Freedom and requests the people of the Frontier Province not to let the memory of the martyrs be obliterated from the minds. The names of the Mrtyrs will be written in golden words in the history of India, and their foot marks will serve as a path for the others. Thre people should continue the struggle for freedom wi non-violence."
d
He then delivered a speech in Pushto saying that it was impossible for any of the nations to achieve its freedom without offering sacrifices. The Khans etc. of this Province were accustomed to attain their objects by begging from the Government but they were always unsuc- cessful as % Government would never give anything if approached in this manner. The Pathans who wore de mother ways considered incapable have, after offering sacri" at Peshawar, Takkar, Utmanzai and Charsadda, proved men selves as capable as any of the other nations of thhers. world. Freedom could not be attained by begging or em 71
passing resolutions. Its pce was the blood of the
£rཀ
B

c_red_shirt_5_pesh_city_1931_p097.text:
(2)
A
youngmen. If the Government gave them anything now, it would not be a favour of her part, as the young- men had paid sufficient price for liberty by shedding their blood at Peshawar, Charsadda, Utmanzai, Takkar and Bannu, Addressing to the people he remarked that that was the price of freedom if they wanted it. They could only get their rights through the blessed blood of the Martyrs. The Frontier Province had been fertilized by the blood of the youngmen,
Nikko Devi delivered a speech in which she reprimanded the young men for the cowardice they had shown in the Qissa Khani Bazar. The incident had compelled her to come on the stage. She regretted that the youngmen who had come out to celebrate the 'Martyrs' Day' proved so timid that on the mere noise of a motor car they ran away imagining that firing had been started. Was this the courage of which they boasted they would free their country? If their lives were so precious, then there was no need of their taking part in a ceremony which had been held in the memory of the 'Martyrs'. The women would never care if the Military and the machine guns would pass over their bodies. The fugitive young men had crushed women and children under their feet in the Qissa Khani Bazar. The 'Martyrs' who were dead were far better than those living martyrs. She concluded with the remark that she had taken an oath not to join any pro- cession of the men in future.
ng it

Allah Bakhsh 'Yusafi' then rose and said that he agreed with what had been said by Nikko Devi. The incident of the Qissa Khani Bazar was the result of the shameful attitude of the youngmen. Nikko Devi should forgive them as a mother should do. The remedy for the cowardice of the young men did not lie in the separation from them of their mothers. The mother should bring them up.properly and show them the right path. They should never separate themselves fr
r

their children. They youngmen had really committed a mistak and had trampled the women under their feet. He supported the above resolution.
S
Abdul Rab Nashtar' said that the occurrence in the Qissa Khani Bazar had caused him a great shock, and he was unable to deliver a speech in a proper manner. The people and some shameless youngmen had given a proof of their cowardice, but the 'Red-shirts' stood firm and unmoved like the Himalayas. India was a slave country and slavery was the cause of the incident of the Qissa Khani Bazar. There was no need of raising shouts of 'Inqilab Zinda Bad' and Government Barbad' as liberty could not be attained thus. They should organize themselves and keep themselves ready for offering sacrifices. He was not so sorry at their cowardice, as those who could not do so much as to wear Khaddar, what else could be expected of them. He asked them to do some practical work, and to promise that from that day they would not wear foreign co cloth. By wearing country made clothes the economical condition of the country could be improved. Those who could not wear Khaddar could not face shooting. All should promise to prepare themselves for the struggle in a peaceful way. If God excused them, Nikko Devi would also excuse. The foreign clth dealers should not be compelled to stop their trade. If people gave up the use of foreign cloth, the dealers XXX would auto- matically stop to deal in it. He also remarked that the wife of Sardar Ganga Singh had sent a message that she shared in ir the grief for the Martyrs.
it
Maulvi Abdul Hakim said that that was the day of mourn ing. All should act on what the speakers had said. The reso0
lution mentined above was passed unanimously.
