C. I. D. Daily Diary
Dated
27/11/36
CONGRESS ELECTIONEERING OR PAGARA. It is reliably reported Chat Abdul Shaffer Khan has Instructed Dr Khan Sahib to take Sardar Vallabhai Patel and labai Dogal to aripur, Abbottabad, ansehra and Beffe, without fail.
At a secret meeting of aide-1-hidantgars held on the 25th Nova aber Dr Khan Sahib was urged to impress on the Deputy Commissioner that, without a bund, the procession to be taken out on the occasion of the visit of Vallabhai Patel and label Bosal would look like a funeral procession. He was asked to do his best to convince the Deputy Commissioner that the procession would be a peaceful one and that there would, therefore, be no objection to a band accompanying . Or Khan Sahib promised to do his boat, during his interview with the Deputy Commissioner on the 27th November. Printed Pashita posters signed by Arbab Abdul Ghafor appealing to the public to join the procession and hootings which will be organised in honour of ardar Vallabhai Patol and Bulebei Desai, have been placarded end distributed in Washower City.

It was after Mr. Jinnah's visit that he applied, and was granted permission, to come to the Province. This permissio assured him that there would be no interruption in the progra because there would be nothing unlawful in it. He would say that nothing less than complete independence could be called "Independence" (Choors). The Indian National Congress stood for "Complete Independence" enjoyed by other countries and Indians should not be ashamed of standing by their land as they had had enough of slavery. The second message of Congress was that no nation could live unless it understood and noted up to the massage of the lend which was its birthright. Let all at once recognise the sense of equality, humanity and national brotherhood. Many a time had they been told by Englishmon that the numerous religions were barrier in the way of freedon. The real barrier was their own narrow-mindedness and cowardico. An English friend, who had remarked that the many religions in India stood in the way of freedom, was referred to the history of his own country where there existed a constant conflict between Protestants and Roman Catholics. Religion was no barrier. It was a link between God and man, and had nothing to do with politics or national power. Every Indian should think in terms of his motherland for much had become part and parcel of his very being (Cheers).

It was the mission of Congress to preserve the resources md wealth of India. In connection with the pronouncement of His Excellency the Viceroy, he said that Indians need not be taught to drink milk but should be provided with means to buy and drink milk.
The last message of Congress was its implicit faith in feeedom and non-violanco. Truth and non-violence would lead to the freedom of the country.
Concluding, he said that anyone who refused to join Congress was unfit to live in this land (India) which was their birthright.
Dr.Ghosh thanked the speaker for th his "illuminating lecture" and the audience for their patience in listening. Ho asked all present to support Congress.
Dr.Khan Sahib said that he would appeal to ell to act on the advice of the speaker (Mr Desai). He also emphasised the support of Congress.
Noto.-Though the speech was well received, some Muhamadans were heard remarking, later, that Mr Desai should net matters right in Bombay which at the moment was the scene of ugly communal riots.
After the above speech by Desai, Sardar Vallabhai Patel, Bhulabai Desai, Mani Bon, Dr.Khan Sahib and a few others left for Charsadda and Utmanzai, and addressed a meeting, the details of which have not yet been received.
This morning (the 30th November) the party went to Jamrud. On their return they intend proceeding to Kohat and thence to Abbottabad.

Reception of Congress leadersA special meeting of ex-redshirts was held in the shop of Dr. Khan Sahib on the night of the 24th November to draw up a programme for the reception of Vallabhai Patel and Bhulabai Desai. It was decided that redshirt uniforms should not be worn. All present agreed that the Leaders should be taken in proces- sion from the railway station to the city and that a public meeting should be held in the Shahi Bagh on the afternoon of the 28th November.
The following sub-committees were formed :-
(1) Arch Committee.
(2) Volunteer Corps Committee.
(3) Public Meetings Committee.
(4) Poster Committee.
(5) Lodging and Boarding Committee.
(6) Transport Committee.
Arrival of Congress leaders in PeshawarSardar Vallabhai Patel, Mr. Bhulabai Desai and Miss Mani Ben, daughter of Mr. Patel, arrived at the Peshawar city railway station by the Bombay Express on the morning of the 28th Novem- ber. A crowd of about 200 persons, including the following ex- redshirts, received them at the station :-
Dr. Khan Sahib.
Dr. C. C. Ghosh. Qazi Ataullah, Pleader. Samin Jan, Pleader.
Abdul Qaiyum, Bar-atLaw.
Ghulam Muhammad Khan of Lunkhwar.
Ali Asghar, Pleader.
Amir Muhammad Khan of Hoti.
Masam Khan of Takkar. Arbab Abdul Ghafur of
Tehkal.

Khan.

General
Majlis-i-Ahrar.

Acharaj Ram Ghamandi. Roshan Lal.

On detraining, the leaders were welcomed by Dr. Khan Sahib and Mussammat Nikko Devi (a terrorist suspect), and profusely garlanded.
Ghulam Muhammad alias "Gama "shouted Sardar Patel, Mr. Desai, Miss Mani Ben, Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Dr. Khan Sahib Zindabad ".
in
one
to
car
Vallabhai Patel, Bhulabai Desai and Dr. Khan Sahib proceeded Peshawar city. In the car from the station following were seated Mussammat Nikko Devi, Miss Mani Ben and Dr. C. C. Ghosh. Five other cars in which local Congressmen were seated, followed.
The cars proceeded at a normal pace to the Hashtnagri Gate where they slowed down. A crowd of about 300-400 persons followed the cars from the Hashtnagri Gate to the shop of Dr. Khan Sahib. Before entering Dr. Khan Sahib's shop, Vallabhai Patel addressed the crowd. He said that he had a message from Abdul Ghaffar Khan and would deliver it at a meeting in the Shahi Bagh.
The Congress leaders were welcomed at the stations given below by the persons noted against each :—
aurleader.
The few Government officials who were present, merely went to hear Desai speak in English. They were disappointed because he spoke in Urdu.
Abdul Qaiyum, Bar-at-Law, introduced the Leaders to the audience and paid a glowing tribute to them.

2/12/36
CITY PARLIAMENTARY BOARD. A source, believed to be reliablo, reports that Abdul Wadud Sarhaddi, Ibrahim Khan of Chacra, Babu Ilahi of Pakha, Musa Khan of Chamkani, Habib Shoh of Nasirpur and a few others met in the office of the City Parliamentary on the 1st December.
In
B-oard in response to Babu Ilhi's suggestion Arbab Abchil Chafur promised to hold protest meetings against the Professional Tax. It was affirmed by the former that voters were insisting on Khuda-i-Khidmatgars doing something. towards the immediate removal of the tax.
Arbab Abdul Ghafur asked Abdul Wadud Sarhaddi to print the statement of Abdul Ghaffar Khan, replying to the Home Member's speech, in the "Shora" and issue posters containing the statement. The latter agreed to do so.

On behalf of the Provincial Parliamen- tary Board, Abdul Qaiyum Khan, Barrister, read an address of welcome to Messrs. Patel and Desai The address eulogized the services of both the leaders in the cause of the country and assured them of the loyalty of Khudai Khidmatgars to the Congress and its principles of non-violence. It condemned the policy of the Goverment
for not remaining neutral in the elections
and in not allowing Abdul Ghaffar Khan to
enter the province and in banning processions in honour of the above two leaders.
A copy of the address in Urdu is attached. Sardar Patel thanked the people for the presentation of the address of welcome. He said that the object of his
and Desai's visit to the Frontier was :-

C. I. D. Daily Diary
Dated
ELECTIONEERING PROPAGANDA
24/12/06
Peshawar Hindu West ConstituencyA meeting attended by about 70 persons was hold in the Shankargarh Dharamsala on the 22nd December, under the presidency of Dr. Jenahir Singh. Prominent amongst those prosent wore Chola Ran Dr C.C. Ghosh Acharaj Ram Chamandi, Ram Saran Datt and Anir Chand alias "Rangin" •
Opening the proceedings, Amir Chand said that the meeting had been convened to advise voters how to voto, also to acquaint them with the work of Congress in the past. It was a pity that the meeting was so poorly attended. It indicated that Hindus of Shankargarh had no love for their country.
Continuing, he said there wore 35 crores of Indians. It was well known that Pathans, Sikhs and Rajputs were brave people, yet India remained a country of slaves. Government had, however, granted them reforms and Congress had decided to support Dr C.C. Chosh in the forthcoming Provincial Assembly elections. The reforms fell far short of their demands as they would have no control over the Defence Department, the Police, the Revenue Department and the Income-tax Department. Dr C.C.Chosh, if returned, would endeavour to put matters right. His opponents, if asked what their programme would be, would reply that they would support Govern ent. Dr C.C. Ghosh had been to jail. His opponents, on the other hand, had grown thxx fat at the expense of the poor. Acharaj Ram Chamandi said that their General, Dr C.C. Chosh, was being opposed by capitalists whose object was to ruin a pity that the children of the poor went about naked while capitalists dressed in silk and enjoyed life. Voters should support suitable persons only at the polls.
Roshan Lal, son of Mussamaat Nikko Devi, said that Dr C.C.Ghosh's opponents were contesting election with the sole object of shaking hands with Sahibs. Dr C.C.Ghosh would work differently as he would endeavour to prevent the ruination of the country.
In his second speech, Amir Chand alias "Rangin", said it pained him to see the children of the poor wandering about naked. Dr C.C.Ghosh's opponents were Government contractors and were not worthy of support.
Dr C.C. Ghosh said that he had been living in Poshawar for the past 20 years. He once opened a night-school in which the children of the poor were being educated. He had opposed the Rowlatt A-ct and was, in consequence, externed to Mandalay. Although Congress had decided to contost election all agreed that the reforms were disappointing. In obedience to the orders of the Parliamentary Board he was contesting the Peshawar Hindu West Constituency. His opponents were wealthy and were seeking election for personal motives. He, on the contrary, would always oppose Government and work in the interests of the country. If elected, he would work for the cancellation of the Hindi-Gurmukhi Circular.

The reason wes not far to soo. A split occurrod in the Hindu Sabha over the nomination of candidates. The Hindu-Silch Bationalist Party won thenformed and nonbers of the Sabha wore permitted to support any candidate of their own choice. Proceading, he said that Indians only were the rightful Overs of India. Anglishman co to India as traders lut soon began to interfere in the administration of the country. Disturbances arose and Englialmen, being victorious, treated Indians as a conquered race. In 1860 the were made couards, was pasuod. In 1997, by which indiens 1857 Muhamadans took part in the Mutiny and incurred the displeasure of Government. Bir Bold Alumad Khan accordingly founded the Aligarh College and placated Government. In 1635 an Englion by the name of Sir William Cotton founded Congress and advised indians to launch a non-violent campaign. In those days Congress was different to the Congress of today. During the Christmas vacations Indians of note met and passed resolutions in order to earn the approval of Government. Tilak actually called the Congress of those days An Assembly of Crows". In 1907 there was a split when Congress mot under the proaidongy of Rash Behari lose. Parties known as Extremists and odorates were formed but thero was little improvement. The President of Congress was invariably made a Judge of the High Court. On the return of Mahatza Gandhi from South Africa, however, the policy of Satyagrahia was decided on. Later, in 11, the Prime Minister of England announced that india would be given Swaraj if it assisted in the Great War. Mahatma Gandhi assisted the British but was given nothing. A public meeting attended by about 200 persons was held in the Kotangar Mosque, "D" Division, on the 9th October, under the presidency of Maulana Abdul Rahim.
Mian Abdullah Shah of Mazara said that Parliamentary Boards had succeeded in educating the public in election affairs. It was the foremost duty of every Muhammadan to strive for freedom. Death It was was the right punishment for those who refused to do so. after Sir Norman Bolton had, on the advice of Khans, refused to grant reforms to the province that Abdul Ghaffar Khan initiated the redshirt movement. Later, Government were satisfied that Pathans deserved reforms and that Khans had deceived them.
Proceeding, he said that during the absence of redshirts in jails title-holders were returned to Council. It was due to them that Abdul Ghaffar Khan had been externed from the province. The Khanate class had no sympathy with the public and worked for their own ends. Voters should return such persons who would work for a reduction of revenue and other taxes, to Council.
Maulvi Abdul Qaiyum said that God had decreed that Muham- 975 madans should not follow anybody who thirsted for their blood. Nationalists should be supported at the polls.
Qazi Habib-ul-Rahman said that voters should carefully con- sider to whom they should give their votes as they would be approach- ed by many imposters.
to
Proceeding, he said that there were many Members of the Labour Party in England who sympathised with Indians. It was on the advice of the Labour Party that Dr. Ansari advised Congress to contest elections. In entering legislatures their object was repeal repressive laws, especially the Public Tranquillity Act, under which Maulana Abdul Rahim had been restricted. Parliamentary Board nominees would work for the repeal of repressive laws and should be supported.
Abdul Qaiyum, Bar-at-Law, said that in 1937 the New 969 Constitution would come into force and grant them liberty. The Governor, however, would have immense powers and could, by using them, impede the attainment of liberty.
Speaking on District Board elections, he said that polling would take place on the 30th October. Many Government pensioners were standing for election and many Parliamentary Board nominees were also doing so. With public support Parliamentary Board nominees would be returned.
Referring to the forthcoming Council election, he said that Parliamentary Board nominees would be opposed by Government men. The Board's nominees would work for the repeal of repressive laws, agitate for the release of political prisoners, demand education for the poor and endeavour to reduce the pay of high .officials.
Concluding, he referred to Abdul Ghaffar Khan's exclusion from the province, the banning of Khuda-i-Khidmatgar processions, and Government's insincerity with regard to its neutrality in election affairs.
Arbab Abdul Ghafur of Tehkal said that the coming reforms 965 were the result of the sacrifices of the poor. If they had their own Government foreign nations would not be able to rule over them. Khans were obstacles on the path leading to liberty. A person could not be a Khan Bahadur or Khan Sahib without doing ." zulum "

PESHAWAR CITY PARLIAMENTARY BOARD.-
A meeting attonded by
about, 180 Hindus, 50 Sikhs and 20 Muhammadans, including the following, was held in Dharamsala Hairajji, under the presidency
of Dr Khan Sahib on the 5th November.-
Gaim Shah, pleader, Dr. C.C.Ghosh,
Arbab Abdul Ghefur of Tehkel Ran Singh of Bannu,
Ishwa Matter of Bannu, Amrit Kaur of Jullundur, Musstt. Nikko Devi. JRoshan Lal,
Chela Ram,
Mehta Kirpa Ram,
Acharaj Ran Ghamandi, Ram Saran Datt
Bohari Lal, s/c Kishen Chand, Jawala Das,
Dilbagh Rai,
Madan Lal, Pleader,
J Raghu Nath, Pleader.
Maden Lal, Pleader, said that membership of the Parliamentary Board was open to all communities. Branches of the Boerd had been established in districts, tahsils and villages. All Boards were sub-ordinate to the All-India Congress Parliamentary Board. The Provincial Parliamentary Board had decided to contest all seats in the forthcoming Assembly elections. The reason why Congress had decided to contest elections was that in the past unsuitable persons, with no national service to their credit, were elected and thereafter encouraged Hindu-Muslin disunity. It was in 1934 that the decision to contest elections was made. Dr Khan Sahib was elected to the Assembly in spite of his being

c_red_shirt_6_pesh_dist_1936_p066.text:
Ras Singh of Ben said that the Frontier Province hai set an example of national service which was envied by mercy countries. Only Congress could guide the country aright. Six or seven years previously, whenever reforms were demanded, they wore told that Pathans were unfit. It was through the efforts of Congress that the Frontier Province was placed on a with other genxkukka provinces of indin. it was due to the rifices of ex-redshirts that the change was brought about. As Congress 414 not contest the first elections unsuitable persona were elected and the public auffored badly. Government Eook the opportunity of passing repressive laus with the assistance of representatives of the public. Congress accordingly decided to content all elections and remove the stigma of inferiority. they Congress nominees would not agok election for personal gain. Their object would be to wreck the law Constitution, if elected. Voters should, therefore, support Khuda-1-Khi atgars. Only Indians who know their country were entitled to frame 1.
Proceeding, Ram Singh said that sitting Members of Counoll had passed a law more servers than an Ordinoo. It was strange that haslim M.L.Cs had opposed the law and Hindus and Sikhe supported it. Under that law ikko Devi, Jana Das, Isher Das, Bhagat Ren of Challeder (Terrorists), whose only offence was that they were related to Hari Kishan, had been rootricted to opacified areas. Under the s80 law Dr Satya Pal had been prohibited from entering the Province and Abdul Chaffar Khan had been externed from the nk province. In other countries porsons could move about as they wished. It was a pity that Indians had not the same liberty in their own country. In order that much lens might be repealed voters should support Khudo-1-Khidmatgars.
Amrit Kaur of Jullundur said that she had served the public for the Test 17 years. It has always been her wish to visit the Frontier and it had been fulfilled when she attended the Sewa Samiti Conference in Dera Ismail Khan. When there, she was invited to an end had come to Peshawar to see the country of the "Sarhaddi Gandhi". She would have been delighted if the "Sarhaddi Gandhi" had been present. He was a great man who had served his country well.
Proceeding, she advised the public to support nominees of the Parliamentary Board in the forthcoming elections. Discipline, she said, was necessary. It was essential that all should oboy their General, 1.0. the Parliamentary
Axx Board. Hindus, Sikhs and uhamadans seeking election to the Assembly for personal gain should not bo mupported. The public should work as a teas in the service of their country. Bone Hindus were opposed to Congress, believing that nothing had been done to wond the Communal Award. Such propaganda was felse.
Proceeding, she said that Indians did not aparently like home-made articles. Indian graduates always sought Government appointments and considered beneath their dignity to work as labourers. They were slaves of fashion and, as such, committed suicide. It would be botter for the to serve their country. Those who died in the service of their country were fortunate.
Contimin, she said that Indian women had made no progress whatever. She would like them to read the stories of brave peoples. Indie was, however, a poor country. She had seen with her omni eyes a woman burn her son because she could not support him. In order to stamp out poverty work was necessary. Japan and other countries had become rich because Indians wore fond of foreign-made articles. Poverty would disappear if Indians would give up using foreign goods. Liberty was only possible by unity. Muhamadans wore clamouring for the return of the Shahid Ganj Mosque, and were saying that

c_red_shirt_6_pesh_dist_1936_p067.text:
the big templo
of liber-) ty after Wh
they would be
able to take
possos- sion of
3they would take possession of the Gurdwara na woll. She would like Indians first to gain possession of their own mosques, temples, etc. Speaking on the Hindi-Gurmukhi Circular, she said thet Muhammadans should bear in mind that there was a possibility of the toaching of Urdu being proscribed in the past. Hindus should transact all their business in Hindi and Gurmukhi and engage privato servants with a knowledge of the two languages. Concluding, she said that her visit to Peshawar had brought homo to her that in the Frontier males wors free. Pathans should do something to free their females. They should not be kept in "pardah". Dr Khan Sahib said that the audience were fortunato in having heard the speech of Amrit Kaur. If Pathans would visit other provinces they would get to know that many women had suffered imprisonment in the service of their country. It was necessary, in order to attain liberty, that workers should suffer prisonment. Those who held a contrary view were wrong. When he was in jail he realised that Indians would never attain liberty so long as they kept their women in "pardah". The "Pardah" system was the cause of many childron contracting consumption. Pathans were not justified in keep- ing their women in "pardah" while they themselves shrieked for liberty. Liberty could not he attained by religious or seditious speeches.
Proceeding, Dy Khan Sahib said that members of the Parliamentary Board were not working for titles. Auda-i- Khidmatgars would serve Hindus, Silds and Muhammadans alike and support suitable persons only in the elections. The old should pivo way to youth. Young men should work as a team to drive out poverty and slavery.

the same. Abdul Qayum asked &&da to

Madan Lal Pleader in a short speech explained that the Parliamentary Board Peshawar was part and parcel of the
All India Con ress Committee. Its object
was to send its own representatives to
the legislative bodies to shut out

-6undesirable element, which passed repressive laws, which retarded the gDowth of mtional movement. He appealed to the voters to vote for the Parliamentary Board nominees in the elections. The Board nominees, he said, would serve their cause.
Ram Singh of Bannu praising the work of the Peshawar District Parliamentary Board said, that they had always bern giving a lead in public affairs and if they continued to lead the province in such a manner, they would secure a final victory. They should all support congress. The congress boycotted the last elections and now they were contesting them..There were reasons for that. By the bboycott of the Congress undesirable elements entered the Councils and acting as puppets in the hands of the CCGovernment, passed laws repressing the national movement. In order to remove pethe bar the congress has decided to
capture the gislatures, and check the inanti-national forces from doing further
tt
mischief. He said, that due to the boycot thof the Council at the last elections the UN.W.F.P. Public Tranquillity (Additional poPowers) Act, was passed in 1932 in the local legislature. By the provisions foof that act, their leaders like Abdul 10 Ghafar Khan and Dr.Satya Pal were prohibited from entering the Province.
In order to smash these laws, he said, it
was necessary, that they should rise
above

c_red_shirt_6_pesh_dist_1936_p072.text:
-7113
above communal bias and return national representatives. Fimally he appealed to the voters to vote for Congress-redshirt nominees in the elections.
Bibi Amar Kaur, said, it &A&A was
a matter of great pleasure and honour
for her, that she was present in the holy land of the Frontier Gandhi. She asked those present to follow the lead of the Frontier Gandhi and serve the counry. They should learn discipline. They should learn to face hardships with courage. They should not fear death, as it was an inevitable thing. They should respond to the call of the nation and jump into the field, held by the Congress fearlessly They should give their power of attorney to the Congress and should not be led by communal leaders on sentimental appeals to religion. In the end, she asked the people to give vocational training to their youngmen to relieve unemployment in the country. She also asked them to patronise Swadeshi goods. She condemned the anti-Hindi Gurmukhi Circular and asked the people to undo its bad effect by popularising Hindu and Gurmukhi.
Dr.Khan Sahib thanked Bibi Amar Kaur for her learned address. He asked voters to vote for the real representatives
of the people in the elections and not to
be misled by Rai Bahadurs or Khan Bahadurs, who acted according to their masters'
voice. He appealed to young men to remove
poverty and slavery from among them.

Raghu Nath, Pleader.
Madan Lal, Pleader, said that membership of the Parliamentary Board was open to all communities. Branches of the Board had been established in districts, tahsils and villages. All Boards were subordinate to the All-India Congress Parliamentary Board. The Provincial Parliamentary Board had decided to contest all seats in the forthcoming Assembly elections. The reason why Congress had decided to contest elections was that in the past unsuitable persons, with no national service to their credit, were elected. Those persons later encouraged Hindu-Muslim disunity. It was in 1934 that the decision to contest elections was made. Dr. Khan Sahib was elected to the Assembly in spite of his being opposed by two Government
supporters.
Ram Singh of Bannu said that the Frontier Province had set an example of national service which was envied by many countries. Only Congress could guide the country aright. Six or seven years previously, whenever, reforms were demanded, they were told that Pathans were unfit. It was through the efforts of Congress that the Frontier Province was placed on a par with other provinces of India. It was due to the sacrifices of ex-redshirts that the change was brought about. As Congress did not contest the first elections unsuitable persons were elected and the public suffered badly. Government took the opportunity of passing repressive laws with the assistance of "representatives of the public ". Congress accordingly decided to contest all elections and remove the stigma of inferiority. Congress nominees would not seek election for personal gain. Their object would be to wreck the New Constitu- tion, if elected. Voters should, therefore, support Khuda-i- Khidmatgars. Only Indians who knew their country were entitled to frame laws.
Proceeding, Ram Singh said that sitting Members of Council had passed a law more severe than an ordinance. It was strange that Muslim M. L. Cs. had opposed the law and Hindus and Sikhs supported it. Under that law Nikko Devi, Jamna Das, Ishar Das, Bhagat Ram of Ghalladher (Terrorist), whose only offence was that they were related to Hari Kishan, had been restricted to specified areas. Under the same law Dr. Satya Pal had been prohibited from entering the province and Abdul Ghaffar Khan had been externed from the province. In other countries persons could move about as they wished. It was a pity that Indians had not the same liberty in their own country. In order that such laws might be repealed voters should support Khuda-i-Khidmatgars.
Mussammat Amrit Kaur of Jullundur said that she had served the public for the last 17 years. It has always been her wish to visit the Frontier and it had been fulfilled when she attended the Sewa Samiti Conference in Dera Ismail Khan. When there, she was invited to Bannu and had come to Peshawar to see the country of the "Sarhaddi Gandhi ". She would have been delighted if theSarhaddi Canhi " had been n...

C. 1 DAILY DAY, the 14th November 1938
SAXX PARLIAMENTARY BOARD
A public meeting attended by about 600 persons, mostly villegers visiting Peshawar on Friday, was held in the Mohabat hon Mosque after Jums prayers on the 13th November, under the Presidency of Ashiq Shah of Deh Badan Prominent amongst those present were Dr. Khan Sahib, Abdul niyun Brent-law, Arbab All hefur of Tehkel, wir Muhamad Khan of Hoti and Mehdi man, M. C Amir Muhammad Khan, es usual, condeaned Government for not suspending the powers of Honorary ingiatmtes prior to end during the District Board elections. He accused Honor ry ingin trates of using their positions to influence voters.
Abdul niyum, Bar-at-Law, said that he would contest the Provincial Assembly elections on a Khuda-i-hidantgar ticket. The aim of his marty was complete independence. He was a indrul of the heavy responsibilities attached to a Mber of the Assembly. It was useless persona indulging in idle talk. Practical work was necessary as the next elections would be held within three months. It was the duty of voters to suport Rude-1-hidatgere as they had pledged themselves to serve the poor and the nation. Government had announced that every party would contest the next elections, and that there would be no official interference. The orders of Government, however, were not being carried out in the true spirit. It was true that Khuda-i-Khidantgars had been very successful in the District Board elections. They lost three seats, however, owing to the pressure brought to bear on weters by Honorary Magistrates. If Government were neutral what need was there to prohibit procession The banning of processions indicated that Coverment were not neutral in the true sense.
Continuing, Abdul alyum said that Government wished for the co-operation of all parties. hu-1-hidmatgars would never Co-operate till complete independence was attained. If Government were to announce that india would be granted Dominion Status, as granted to Australia, Khuda-i-khidmatgars would at once co-operate. Khud-1-Khidmatgars had one aim only, viz to serve the nation. It was the duty of Goverment to arrange for the education of poor children. Such arrangements will be made should voters support Khude-1-Khidman tga re at the polls. Mehdi Zaman, M. L. C., said that "God helps those who help theas elves". Anation which did not help itself would not be assisted by any other nation. In times gone by politics were discussed in mosquee. It was a pity that Muhammadans had given up attending moaques. Political discussion was necessary. A person who eschewed politics was an enemy of his religion,
Proceeding, he said that there would be no nominated members in the Provincial Assembly. sitting Members of the Legislative Council had done nothing to reduce revenue rates. The Frontier Government received a subvention of one crore from the Government of India and, in addition, realised 17 lakhs within the province. If voters would support suitable persons the money would be utilised for the good of the public. Referring to the speeches in the Legislative Council on a resolution recommending that the ban on Abdul Ghaffar Khen's movements be remo ved, he said that he could see no reason why Government had not agreed to the resolution.
Concluding, he advised woters to support hude-1-Khidmatgars at the polls.
Arbab Abdul Ghefur Khan of Tehkal eulogised the services of Mehdi man khan in the Legislative Council. He said that there were a numbers of L. Cs, unlike Mehdi Zaman, who were prepared to crush the nation. One such was Arbab Muhammad Sharif Khan, who in order to please the lose Member, had opposed the resolution recommending the lifting of the ban on Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Abdul Ghaffar then was not guilty of any offence. He was
a humble Khuda-1-hidna tgar whose desire was to serve the nation. Continuing, Abdul Ghefur said that members of other parties would not prevent an increase in the police force, oppose fresh

Following the removal of the flag Arbab Abdul Ghafur, Bahadur Khan of Tehkal, Ibrahim Khan of Chagra, Abdul Wadud Sephaddi, Abdul Ahad of Takhtabad and a few others met in the shop of Dr. Khan Sahib and discussed the incident.

No.........
Dated........
18/10737
Pandit Jawahar Lal Nehru's tour
978. Interest during the week was centred on the arrival in Peshawar of Pandit Jawahar Lal Nehru. At meetings in and around 9 Peshawar, speakers asked their audiences to assist in giving a suit- able welcome to Jawahar Lal Nehru and bands of redshirts paraded through villages surrounding Peshawar asking the inhabitants to join in the welcome.
Pandit Jawahar Lal Nehru arrived on the morning of the 14th. He was welcomed at Khairabad, Jehangira, Akora, Nowshera, Pabbi, Taru and Nasirpur by crowds varying from about 30 at Nasirpur to over 2,000 at Nowshera. At all these places, except at Khairabad, people in red clothes were in evidence. At the Peshawar City Railway Station a crowd estimated between 15 and 20 thousand had collected to greet him. In this crowd were represented parties of all shades of political opinion, the greatest in number being the redshirts (1,500) and the Khaksars (300). The Pandit was taken in a procession about a mile long through the main bazars of the city, where the numbers swelled to about 30,000.

c_red_shirt_7_pesh_city_1937_p113.text:
978-contd.
paragraphs. Previous
In the afternoon he addressed a meeting of about 30,000 people, under the presidency of Abdul Ghaffar including 1,500 in red clothes, which was held in the Shahi Bagh Khan. Pandit Jawahar Lal 956 Nehru thanked the audience for
been accorded to him and said he was unable to express his thanks in mere words. Although he lived some 2,000 miles from the Frontier, he was in complete sympathy with the people of this Province, whom he was visiting in furtherance of his object of obtaining liberty for India for which so many brave people had made sacrifices. He had travelled extensively throughout India and during the course of his travels had come to realise that it was the Indians' desire for liberty which had prompted them to free them- selves from the bondage of Englishmen. He realized that great bravery and many sacrifices were necessary to attain this freedom but in addition wisdom and brains were essential. Without these their endeavours would easily be brought to nothing. It was no easy matter to attain liberty for India, and to be successful it was essential that all Indians should work in unity in order to free them- selves from the slavery to which, he regretted, a large number of them had become accustomed. This Province had a number of fine leaders but revolution was not brought about by a few chosen people. He alleged that the British Government and certain leaders used the factor of communal ill-feeling for their own ends and when necessary fanned the flames of ill-feeling for their own ulterior motives. He asked the audience to give up fighting between themselves. The liberty for which they were striving was the liberty of all communities and was not a question of liberty for one community and slavery for another. Realising this, Congress from the very beginning had allowed all its members full religious and social liberty. The strength of a nation depended not so much on its numerical strength as upon its national strength. Had this not been true India would never have been in slavery. A number of people were mistaken as to the meaning of Swaraj. They thought that it meant the replacement of Englishmen by Indians. This was not the correct interpretation. Swaraj meant a complete change in the method of administration and even if Indians held all appoint- ments from the Governor downwards this would not be Swaraj as the system of administration would be the same as at present. That was why they had attained little by the new reforms and why he was not satisfied with the success of Congress in seven provinces in India. It was true that they had obtained a certain degree of power but the system of administration was still the same. There could be no real liberty while British troops were in India and while they were governed by a handful of Englishmen. In the background were the capitalists in England for whose benefit India was being run. Congress had opposed the new Constitution and they had now only accepted office as a means of wrecking it. He criticised the British Government's policy in Palestine and considered that, as in India, they were oppressing the poor. He thought that another serious European war was imminent and when it broke out repercussions would be felt in India. Congress would oppose the employment of Indian troops in any war in which Great Britain might involve herself as it was unfair that India should suffer on account of Great Britain's aspirations. The aim of Congress was to drive out the British Government from India and they should not therefore assist in strengthening Great Britain's imperialism by assisting her in a war. He referred to the recent despatch of troops to China and said that in reality they had been despatched to safe- guard the interests of British traders though the Government had given out that they had been sent to protect Indians. He criticised Government's policy in Waziristan and deprecated the dropping of bombs on defenceless people. He maintained that the fact that

c_red_shirt_7_pesh_city_1937_p114.text:
978-concid. Previous
Government had been unable to solve the Frontier question in 80 years was a definite proof of their incompetency. Had Congress been allowed to interfere, this Tribal question, with the assistance of Abdul Ghaffar Khan, would have been settled long ago.
In conclusion he appealed to the audience to support Congress and to open branches in every Mohalla and every village. He appreciated the sacrifices which Pathans had made and asked them to continue the struggle even if their leaders were taken away from them.
Later in the evening he addressed another meeting, which was attended by about 4,000 people and which was held on the Sadar Polo Ground in Peshawar Cantonments. The speech which he made was similar to that reported above.
sons to
On the morning of the 15th he addressed a meeting of about 350 women, which was held in the Arya Girls School in Peshawar City. He reminded them that much depended on the women of India in their struggle for freedom. He asked them to induce their join the Congress movement and appealed to them to wear Khadar and to discard the custom of wearing fashionable clothes. Later in the day, accompanied by Abdul Ghaffar Khan, the Hon'ble Minister for Education and other M. L. As., he visited Shabqadar, where six to seven thousand people had assembled to welcome him. He was taken in procession through the bazar but to Utmanzai, addressed no meeting. From Shabqadar he went which had been suitably decorated for the occasion, and addressed a meeting of about 3,000 people in the Azad School. He expressed his pleasure at the opportunity afforded to him of visiting the Frontier Province and said that although Utmanzai was only a small village it was known throughout India for the sacrifices which its inhabitants had made in the cause of freedom. Complete freedom, he was sure, would be attained but possibly not in the lifetime of
all those present.
After spending the night in village Guli Garhi, he returned to Peshawar on the morning of the 16th, visited the Khyber Pass and later in the day addressed meetings in the Islamia College and Edwardes Mission College and left Peshawar by the Frontier Mail on the morning of the 17th.

e
adopted:-
) That the Government should release the Garhwali prisoners of 1930,

(c) That the Frontier Ministry should abolish the Afghan Shirkats
monoply on dry fruits, etc.
(d) That the ass ociations affiliated to the City Congress Committee should not act on any schemes without approval of the City Congress Committee,
(e) That the Government should appoint a Committee to look after the d welfare of the Frontier Jails prisoners.
(f) That the Government should
re-instate all men removed from Government service in connection with the Civil Disobedience and Non-Co-Operati on Movements and restore to them their confiscated property and arms
(g) That the Peshawar Municipality should revise bye-laws and
reduce Octroi duties and arrange supply of water in elevated places round the Gorkhatri.

c_red_shirt_7_pesh_city_1937_p208.text:
PESHAWAR CANTONMENT CONGRESS O TEA
Dian Singh alias Zinda Shaid of Nawan Shar District Hazero addressed a meeting of about 50 persons in Chauk Bazar Peshawar Cantt., on the evening of 20th December.
During the course his speech he said that capitalists and poor people were the two classes which constituted the population of India. The former vore enjoying and had all amenities of life at their disposal while the latter wore illclad, were straving, and shivering with cold. Capitalists were sucking the blood of poor people and using every thing English. Enkan England rated urned woro lucky enough to secure Government service and use every thing foreign. Sometime they bring English lady with thon. and dine with them. So to speak such people are Government agents and the other agents are titled holders who also use foreign things and induce others to do the same. Proceeding the speaker said that such persons promote communal strifes and their hobby was to keep all communities apart in order to strengthen the foreign Government as they were under the impression that they would loose all honours if the British Government were to go away.
Continuing the speaker appealed to the audience to use Swadashi and not to be hoodwinked by toadies and Governments agents who were playing the roll of traitor and xtxtt styling one another as Kaffar in order to serve their own ends.
Speaking on the morcha in the city, he said that he stoutl opposed the idea of launching it as with liberty all such question will be automatically solved, but none heeded his exhortations.
Proceeding he said that now they were under a national Government so they had been given a golden opportunity to organise themselves. Let that all the parties may function separately, but about the question of liberty they all should unite. Liberty will be attained when the English and their agents will go away. After their departure they will be able to conduct their own distribution.
Speaking on redshirts he asked the audience to increase their strength Es in view of their services.
Proceeding he appreciated the activities of Dr. Khan Sahib who was touring the Province in order to study the greivences of people as he had an ardent desire to serve the nation.
A redshirt office the speaker said,hhd been opened in Feshawar Cantt. and he would like all to enroll themselves as redshirts. lindus and Sikhs should not despise red uniform es it was not of ordinary red colour but was dyed in blood of thors who had laid down their lives in Kidsa Khani Bazar and other places.
Proceeding he asked the audience to support congress during the forthcoming election and not bo be lured away by capitalists who mou offen 41---

C.I.D/DAILY DIARY LATER THE 20TH DAGAMRE, 1937
SHALAR CANTONMENT CONGRESS COMMITTA
A meeting attended by about 200 persons
including
six in red clothes, was held in Chowk Bazar Peshawar Cantt: on the night of 28th December, under the residency of Bel-Ray 4. congress flag was hoisted at the place of the meeting
on Unity,
Dhiens ingh alias Pardesi of Marara briefly spoke
Pt: Shiv Dat of Campbellpur, now in Peshawar, spoke on the forthcoming Cantonment Board Election and appealed to the audience to support congress nomineo. Proceeding He eulogised the congress "inistry and said that but for it they would have been saddled with Hasiat Tax. It was due to their disunity that very sorts of taxes had been imposeed upon them and they were still groaning under then
Continuing he condemned enhanced rates of house rent and condemned the propreitors. The rest of his speech ras in the above key.
Comrade Derke Nath recited & poem appealing to all to unite under congress flag in order to gain freedom.
Girdhari Lal of Peshawar Cantonment spoke length on Hindu Muslim Unity. Proceeding he said that Hindus had complaints against Mahmud of Ghazni that he drained their whole wealth to his country, but they did not know that Mohd Ghouri brought everything back to India. Look at the Englishmen, the speaker said, who were draining wealth from India, sometimes on the pretence of loan, and sometimes on some other excuses. In Taxila a huge amount of Jewls, gold and silver armements were excavated and everything was taken to England. India which used to be called 'Sparrow of Gold' was in indigent circumstances now a days. Concluding he advocated unity and maintained that they would remain slaves as long as they would be indulged in mutual warfares.
Gopaldass Kheba speaking next said, that it was undoubtedly correct that Mohammedens invalied India many a times but it was not due to it that Indians were Hindus but they wanted to improve their economic condition. Similarly there were many wars in europe to improve economic affairs. Proceeding he said that the communal tinge was given to History by historians according to the religion they professed. If a German the speaker were to write the history of the Great War he would surely pour forth a lot of nonsence against the English and Russians. Continuing the speaker said that now time was charging as between 1857 and 1903 there were only two communal roits and up to 1914 there were about 5 to 6 but til now some 500 to 600 communal roits had occurred. It was all due to the fact that the economic condition of the country was very depolorable and capitalists were erging on poor labourers to create communal trouble.
Alluding to Hindi-Gureakhi Circular, the speaker seid that Hindusand Mohammad ens began to chanian because of their co-religion on its score, but it was all to gain their own ends Continuing the speaker spoke at length on the present

The following meetings have been reported during the
On the 25th September at Lakki; attendance 50. On the 25th September at Kohat; attendance 200.
On the 26th September at Kaki; attendance 100. On the 27th September at Kaki; attendance 400. On the 29th September at Lakki; attendance 300.
On the 30th September at Bazar Ahmad Khan; attendance
On the 1st October in Peshawar City (after Juma prayers) attendance 1,000 (200 in redshirt uniform).
On the 1st October in Peshawar (in the evening) ; attend- ance 4,000 (100 in redshirt uniform).
On the 2nd October at Balakot, District Hazara; attendance 350.
All the speeches made at these meetings have been moderate in tone and for the most part have been confined to praise for the Congress Ministry and advocation of public support for the Congress Party. Comparisons of the work done by the present Ministry and of that performed by the old have been a general feature of speeches and much propaganda in an attempt to gain public sympathy for the Ministry's action in cutting the additional grant to the Islamia College has been indulged in. This action of the Ministry has been seized upon by their opponents and from reports received there is no doubt that Muslims in general are opposed to the cut which has
been made.
Another feature of speeches has been the continued advocation of public support for the Police in the execution of their duties and the point has again been stressed that the public, now that Congress is in power, should look upon the Police as their servants and helpers and not as their enemies.
As a result of recent propaganda, Congress influence is report- ed to have greatly increased, especially in the Districts of Hazara and Bannu, where a number of Congress volunteers have been enlisted.
Meetings in Peshawar Cantonments and in Lakki were held on the 2nd October to celebrate the birthday of Mahatma Gandhi.

CONGRESS
C. I. D. Daily Diary 9/10/37
Dated
A meeting, attended by about 500 people, was held in the Murres Brewery Compound, Peshawar Centonmenta, on the night of the 8th October. Kidar Neth Schgel of Lahore (whose arrival in Pesawar was reported in yesterday's diary) considered that the Reforms which they had been given were an insufficient reward for the sacrifices which they had made; he admitted that Congress Ministries were unable to do everything they wanted to for the good of the people. He said that all their troubles were caused by the lack of unity amongst Muslims, Sikho and Hindus and if only the three communities would unite together they would then be able to attain liberty. In 1919, according to the speaker, Muslims had been beaten when they were saying their prayers, If they would unite occurrences such as this could not then occur and they would be on & par with other European countries. People were poor because of the large salaries which were paid to officials and what little money wes left was used in bribery and corruption.
Hernem Singh, alias Baghi, of Hoshiarpur, a Socialist, complained that a mere handful of Englishmen were ruling thousands of ***** Indians. He related how once a sepoy in the Army having xx beaten a mule was produced before the Commanding Officer who said that the sepoy was only worth Rs.14/-/- a month, whereas the mule cost over Rs.200/-. He should in future therefore treat such animals with due respect. It was their duty to strive for their own liberty and not to rest until complete freedom resulted. They had so far been given no real freedom, though he admitted that Englishmen did make friends with the various communities. This, however, meent nothing. Just because a butcher fed his cattle well it did not mean that, he had any love for them and in the end he killed xxx the cattle which he had fed so well. In the same way a quail was given food and some times opium in order to make it right, so Englishmen made one community fight against another.
Nand Lal of Lahore was the next speaker. He said that the allegation that Congress was a Hindu body had been disproved by the happenings in this province where Congress was almost entirely Muslim. As a result of the firing at Jallienwela Bagh and in the Qissa Khani Bazar and the hardships which they had undergone in jail people had now been given freedom of speech. He alleged that the Government made the communities fight amongst themselves and it was on account of this that Englishmen were in power in India. Great Britain had numerous colonies and if another European war broke out she would suffer- irrepairable loss. He therefore appealed to the Audience not to send their sons and brothers to fight in the cause of Great Britain should another war break out. to give up communal disputes and to devote their attention to the lie appealed to them liberation of the country.
He referred to the partition of Palestine and the consequent ruination of the Arabs and said that wherever Englishmen ruled, disunity naturally followed.

N.W.F.P.
Peshawar, 4th October,
passed at a meeting of the Peshawar Cantonment Congress Committee held on October 4th 1na Red
1. This meeting of the Congress Committee, Peshawar Cantonment, strongly condemns the action of three British soldiers of the H.L.I. Regiment who, after the conclusion of the public meeting held on Octobe: 2nd, stood in front of the Northern Motors premises with the buttons of their pents open and made obscene demonstrations while Indian ladies were pas sing that way.
2. This meeting places on record its sense of appreci -tion of the timely help given by Messrs R.N.Seth, Dewan Roshan Lal, Mohd Amir Khan, and Abdul Karim Khan, Sub-Inspector, who tactfully handled the situation and saved it from taking an ugly turn. 3. This meeting further resolves that the military authorities should be approached with the request to take suitable action against the soldiers concerned who were arrested on the spot and handed
over to the military police.

It le reported that the Subscription Committee has collected about 300/- and intend to collect 200/- more to meet the decoration expenses in connection with the visit of Pt: Jawahar Lal Nehru.
A reliable source reports that the Hindu merchants are Con templeting to present a purse of 2000/- to Pt: Jawahar Lal Nehru.
Brisk arrangements are underway to accord a rousing reception to Pt: Jawahar Lal Nehru end the Reception Committee is holding daily meetings. 280 250
As the Pandit would stay for three days so the Reception Committee intends to draft his tour programme on his arrival but it has been decided that he will address a meeting in Shahi Bagh on the 14th and another in Peshawar Cantonment on 15th. He will also visit The Khyber Pass.
Arbab Abdul hafoor M.L. A will be incharge of the procession. Though opinion in the city is unanimous to accord a
befitting reception to the Pendit but Agha Lel Badshah is making secret efforts that Mohammedens of the city should not join it As the congre se Ministry has cut down the Additonal Grant of the Islamia College.
The Reception Committee has 631gned duties to various th workers to arrange meetings etc.

congressites and non-congressites, inviting the public to Accord a rousing reception to the Pandit, have been distributed in the city (one attached).

C.I.D. DAILY DIARY,dated the 15th October 1937.
PANDIT JAWAHARLAL NEHRU'S VISIT.-
Pandit Jawahar Lal Nehru arrived at the Peshawar City Railway Station by the Bosbay Express on the morning of the 14th October. He travelled 1st class. He was welcomed at Khairabad, Jehangira, Akora
ehera, Pabbi, Taru and Nasirpur by crowds varying from about 30 at Mirpur to over 2000 in Nowshera. At all these places, except Khairabad, people in red clothes were in evidence. Prominent amongst those who welcomed the Pandit at the Peshawar City Railway Station were, the Hon'ble Chief Minister and Abdul Ghafar Khan. All the various parties of Pestiver City were represented, the greatest in number being the redshirts (1500) and the Khaksare (300). A crowd estimated to be between 15 and 20 thousand had collected by the time the train arrived. Outside the station he was saluted by the redshirt and Khaksar parties afer which a procession about a mile in length was formed and entered 1' the city through the Hashtnagri Gate. The crowd swelled to about 30000 in the City. After going through the Rempura Bazar the process18 reached the Clock Tower, where a few people instigated by opponents of the Congress Ministry, were present with black flags. They raised cries! of "go back Nehru", but were quickly removed and their shouts were drowned by the shouts of acculations from the processionists. After proceeding through the issa Khani Bazer, the procession terminated neer the Jail Railway Bridge, from where the Pandit drove to the Hon'ble Chief Minister's bungalow.
In the afternoon, a meeting, attended by about 30000 people, including 1500 in red clothes, was held in the Shahi Bagh, under the presidency of Abdul Ghafer Khan.
Pandit Jawahar Lal. Nehru thanked the audience for the wonderful reception which they had accorded him and said that he could not express his thanks in mere words. Although he lived some 2000 miles from the Frontier, he was in full sympathy with the people of this province and he had come to visit them in connexion with his work of attaining liberty for India in connexion with which so many people had made sacrifices. He had travelled extensively throughout India and during the course of his travels had come to realize that it was the Indians' desire for liberty which had prompted them to free themselves (from the bondage of Englishmen. He apologized to residents of Districts other than Peshawar for his inability to visit them but promised to do so some other time. Great bravery and many sacrifices were necessary to attain the freedom desired by Indians, but in addition wisdom and brain work were essential. Without these their endeavours could easily be destroyed. It was no easy matter to attain liberty for India and to do so it was essential that all Indiens tax should work in unity in order to free themselves from the slavery to which, he regretted, a large number of them had become accustomed.
The Frontier Province had a number of fine leaders. but revolution was not brought about by the speeches of a single person. He maintained that the British Government and certain leaders used the fector of communal ill-feeling for their own ends and when necessary fanned the flames of ill-feeling for their own ulterior me. He asked the audience to give up fighting between themselves. The liberty for which they were striving was the liberty of all communities and w os a question of liberty for one community and slavery for another. It for this reason that from the very beginning the Congress had allowed all its members to have full religious and social liberty. The strength of the nation depended not so much on its numerical strength as upon its national strength. Had it depended on the former, India would never have been in slavery.
A number of people were mistaken as to the meaning of Swaraj. They thought that it meant the replacement of Inglishmen by Indians. This was not the correct interpretation. Swaraj meant a complete chenge of the method of administration and even if Indiens held all the offices from the Governor downwards, this would not be Swaraj as t administration would be the same as at present. That was why they had attained little by the new reforms and by the fact that Congress had formed a number of Ministries. They had obtained a certain degree of power but the system of administration was the same. There could be no liberty so long as there were British Troops in India and while they
5.5.0.

c_red_shirt_7_pesh_city_1937_p087.text:
-2117
were governed by a handful of Englishmen. In the back-ground were the Capitalists in England for whose benefit India was being run. Congress had opposed the new Constitution tooth and nail and hed now BERNIER only accepted office to wreck the constitution. He criticised the British Government's policy in Palestine and onsidered that, as in India, they were oppressing the poor. Another Serious European was was imminent and when it broke out there would repercussions in India. Congress would oppose the giving of assistance to Great Britain by the employment of Indian Troops as it was unfair that India should suffer because Great Britain embroiled herself in a war. The aim of Congress was to drive out the British Government from India and they should not therefore assist in atrengthening Great Britain's Imperialism by assisting her in a war. Me referred to the recent despatch of troops to China and said that in reality they had been despatched to safeguard the interests of British traders though the Government had given out that they had been sent to protect Indians. He criticised Government's policy in Waziristan and deprecated the dropping of bombs on defenceless people He maintained that the fact that, Government had been unable to solve the Frontier question in 80 years was a definite proof of their incompetency. Had Congress been allowed to interfere, with the assistance of Abdul Ghafar Khan, this Tribal question would have bee settled long ago."
In conclusion he appealed to the audience to support Congress and to open branches in every Mohalla and village. He appreciated the sacrifices which Pathena had made and asked them to continue the xxx struggle even if their leaders were taken away from them.
After the meeting those present in red clothes gave a drill demonstration and staged a Khattak dance.
Another meeting, attended by about 4000 people, including 1000 women, was held on the Polo Ground, Peshawar Cantonments, in the evening. Pandit Jawahar Lal Nehru made another speech similar to that which he had made in the afternoon.
This morning the Pandit addressed a meeting of ladies in Peshawar City, details of which are not yet available.
This afternoon he is to visit Shabqader and Utmanzai and tomorrow is going up the Khyber Pass.

A meeting about 30000persons including about 1500 persons in red clothes and the leading congre es leaders of the province was held in Shahi Bagh under the presidency of Abdul Chefar Khan (2.30 to 4.30 p.m.).
The president in his opening speech said that the re ap ear-' ed to be no need to introduce Pandit Jawahar Lal Eehru to the audience as his name wes well known throughout India, we ver he would thank them for the honour to presi de such a meeting and request them to hear the proceedings attentively.

Proceeding he esid that his desire in coming to the Frontier woe to meet his own friends and make new acquaintances. Though he was living at a distance of about 2000 miles but the Frontier blood ren in his veins and the province dragged him towards it and reigned supereme in his hind. His claim was that he woo areocisted to them in close tiel. in addition to it what prompted him much to visit the province was the liberty of India in the attainment of which Indians had made mony sacrifices.
He was opposed to singing of praised because they had a gigantic pieces of work to perform.ne Mith such object in view they met one ano ther to prepare the nation and not to fritter away their time in mutual praises,
Continuing he said that their address contained a brief account of their struggle for liberty and the rushito poem also alluded to them, though he could not understand it fully. However there xx was none in India who did not understand them.
Proceeding he said that in the map of india the Frontier occupied a top place and in the struggle for libe ty it hus re tained it. It wes e source pride for them as well as for the whole of India.
Continuing he said that he visited every nook of India and met trade re, labourers and other people as he wanted to see the country for the freedom of which they were a trugling. His other other object was to discover as to what impelled India to action. He went to Ceylon and several other places which were inhabited by people who spoke different languages and put on afferent costumes. By visiting these places he come to know that it was the desire of liberty which had prompt.d people to free themselves. of Pesiar read two Pushto poems. In
the first one he eulogised the services of Abdul Ghafar Khan in the cause of the Pathans and urged on young and old to follow his lead to liberate the country. In the second he appreciated the spirit of Dr. Khan Sahib in resigning the Ministry under orders of the Central Congress as a protest against the de- claration of H.E. the Viceroy. Comrade Sadhu Singh of Peshawar City read an Urdu poem purporting to explain that the story of the slavery had now come to an end and the poor were to become kings very soon. Hakim Abdul Jalil "Nadvi", in his usual type of speech, expressed sympathy to the sufferers of the Anatolia earthquake and moved a resolution on the subject. He appealed to the Municipal Committee and Congress Committee to help the sufferers with collection of funds. Milap Singh "Azad" speaking in the same strain remarked that had there been a democratic flag in India and had its inhabitants been free they would have sent five or six crores of rupees. It was now the duty of the British to send money to Turkey in aid of the sufferers. Mohd Yunis "Qassab" harping on the same theme condemned the ection of Muslim League in celebrating the "Deliverance Day" and

I. P. s. Daily Diary Peted the 15th January 1940.
Redshirts Peshawar City.
About 95 redshirts in uniform end about 60 others
in plein clothes assembled in Nimak Mandi, A/ Division on the night of the 14th January under the presidency of Captain Khan Mohd.
Addressing them Sheikh Mujahid of Peshawar City, said that every body should note that in the beginning redshirts were in a small number, but they stoutly fought the mechinguns of the British Government who were compelled to raise Additional Police. The Additional Police, he said, contained sons of titleholders who oppressed K.K.vars in every possible manner. However, K.K.ars did not give way and therefore he would
A
request them that they should prove themselves true to their salt.
Continuing, he enjoined upon redshirts to apply themsel- ves to drill and observed discipline as shortly they would be at war again.
The rest of his speech was in the above strain.
Milap Singh Azad speaking rext said that now the time
of speech was over as it was the time of war and they will have
to witness the scenes of 1930 again.
Speaking on non-violence, he said that during the last
50 years congress was carrying on a struggle of non-violence, but in a few days redshirts achieved what others could not get
in many years.
Referring to the propaganda that after the attainment
of liberty 22 crore Hindus would turn out 9 crore Muslims, he said that it was only malicious to side track the main issue before the country.

(iv) A weekly gathering of 160 Redshirts was held in Namak Mandi, Peshawar City, on the night of 14.1.40. Khan Mohd, General,presided. Sheikh Mujahid, Captain appealed to the audience to follow their principles and bear all hardships of Civil Disobedience like the year 1930-31. Milap Singh "Azad" in his usual type of speech emphasised that it was now time for war and not for making speeches. The true Muslimnever fear from any body. The Muslims of Firangi (British) fear from every one. Discussing the Congress pledge os spinning "Charkha", he remarkde that if the Indians used to wear "Khaddar" many factories in Manchester would have closed by this time. Concluding his speech he requested the Government to bring the prices of foodstuffs to previous rate to remove the anxiety of the poor public.

CONGRESS COMMITTEE PESHAWAR CANTONMENT
(INDEPENDENCE DAY).
A meeting attended by about 150
persons as
held in the Cantonment Chork on the night of the 26th January, under the presidency of Mushteg Ahmed.
Shen Lel, Secretary, of the Cantonment Congress Committee, traced the history of the Indpendence Day and said that under the orders of the Indian National Congress the "hole of India was observing the day. Today every Indian was bound to take a vow on the Congress flag that he or she would not take rest until India had become free.
The rest of his speech was in the bove strain and requested the audience to repent the pledge, which would be read by Gopal Dass Khere, in order to show to the British overnment that they would sacrifice their ell for the success of Contress and liberty of India.
Gopal Lass Xhere, said that they were holding
the present meeting in order to afford opportunity to those who had not been able to participate in the Shahi Begh meeting. Proceeding, he said that they were bound to
change the administration which was out to crush them or
deprive them of their right of liberty. The British Government he said, thx had deprived India of its liberty and therefore, they were bound to free it.
Continuing, he said that they all believe that the weapon of violence was not effective to achieve their end as their present strength was due to thee reed of non-violence. He would say that India could become free only by following the cult of non-violence and continued to follow till it had become free. The rest of his speech was in the above strain and he concluded it by reading the pledge.

Milap Singh "Azad", in his usual type of speech, remarked that the Redshirt organisation was formed by Abdul Ghaffar Khan to offer sacrifices for the independence of their country and that's why the colour of their uniform was red. Tracing the past history of the K.K.Gar movement the speaker deplored that the redshirts were indulging in 'Para jamba' and were not united.
Proceeding, he said that they wanted to become free like other nations, but liberty could not be attained unless they adhered strictly to the principles of the movement.
The speaker then alluded to the non-violents fightput
up by the redshirts during the 1930-31 and said that the British- ers were forced to come to an understanding with the redshirts as a result of their sacrifices.
Concluding the sppaker, said to discard evil habits
and to remain united."
Katchkul Khan, Secretary, speaking next alluded to the attrocities excercised by the Britishers, during 1930-31 and said that it was the result of their sacrifices that the Congress had formed ministry in the province, but it was pitiable that the people instead of cooperating with the ministry started putting various blames on the ministers.

Continuing, he said that in 'Ingaleb' lies the solution of all problems confronting to the poor masses, who had to go without meals inspite of hard labour. It was evident that labourers and peasants would be the coming rulers. The speaker further, said that for bringing about 'Inqalab in a country it was necessary that masses should show as many sacrifices as possible, but at the same time, he did not like the idea of coming into a clash with the Britishers with whom they were friendly for more than a century, but at the same time they could not endure that they should suffer poverty and the Britishers should lead & luxarious life. If time did come that when secrifices would be needed, only rodshirts would come to the fore-front.
Proceeding, he criticised the action of the Police in making several arrests under section 34, and suggested that before resorting to moking arrest the Police should notify the fact to poor villagers coming from the rural area by the beat of drum and the Municipal Authorities should also construct unirals at various places in the city.

Referring to the negotiation between Congress and the British Government, he said that they had preotionlly ended. The Enlish, he pointed out did their best to check-mate the people and keep them evey from the real issue, but M. Gandhi tackled the question most shiky ablly.
Proceeding, he criticised the ole ime of the British Govt. for joining the war to nefeguard the liberty of Poland which was

to keep India in bondage if their motives were sincere.

Continuing, he said that in the beginning the British Government stated that India would get Dominion Status after the war, but Indians retarted that they wanted such Dominion Status which should vest them with powers to severe connection with the British Government whenever they liked as Ireland had already done.
Referring to the Bombay speech of the Viceroy, he said that on its basis M.Gandhi met him on the 5th February. He would request the people to sink their differences in view of the promise made by the British vovernment that India will be- if they
coms & dominion after the war. However, he wont unite then they would not get Dominion Status.

abounded in brave Muslim and an brave Rajputs and if the

Dwelling upon Congress demands, he said that one of them was that the system of adult frenchise by enforced and Sir Agha Khan, who was a full fleged agent of the British Empire had endorsed it, but the Government was not in a move to do so. poems.

Referring to the war, he said that when it broke then the British issued a communique that they had joined it to save Poland from the atrocities of Germany and uphold its liberty and also sought the help of India as was done in the last G.War. However, he said that the Contress came out with the assertion that when Britain was going to plunge into the war for the sake of Poland then what was its policy with regards of India which was a bigger country then Poland and approximated even Ehrope
in area. The Government replied that they would like to deal with small powers and then they would tackle with the question of the liberty of India. However, Congress entered

into negotiations with Mr. Jinnha, but they ended in amoko because his party was composed of Knights and title-holders who had spent their whole life in strengthening British Imperialien and gained their own ends.
However, these people did not come to an understanding with Congress because they did not like the deterioration of the British Government, but on the other hand they gave out that if Muslims would strengthen Congress it will bring Hindu Rej in India,
The result of such a nefarious propagenda ves that. many Muslims deviated from the path of the freedom of the country and began to playo obstacles in the way of Congress.
Congress, he said, had committed a blunder of the
highest magnitude since it had accepted the principle of separate electorate because only such Muslims were returned to the Legislature who crushed the spirit of their brothern and hampered the progress of amicable settlement with Congress.
Proceeding, he said that when the Congress gave out that India was one country then Muslim League opposed it Now it was for the people to say how could democracy be established in a country there people held such divergent views. His own views were that as long as British Imperialism held the ground no understanding was possible between the two parties. Congress, he said, came down to the extent that it accepted the illegal demands of the Government to open neotiation with M.League.
Referring to Ram Garh Congress Session, he xx
invited the attention of the audience to Maulana Azad's apeech in Lahore in which he was reported to have said that to free India they will have to fight another peaceful
