As regaris Milap Singh I agree with the view held by D.C. Peshawar that prosecution under dection 124-A I.P.C., or under any other Section of the Pensi Code is not feasible. Milap dinchis ostensibly merely an agent for the sale of newspapers, and it would be difficult to justify his prosecution, even if possible, for the dissemination of seiitious literature or abet. ment of this offence. As regards the action which i have recommended, namely expulsion from the Province, I will put forward my reasons again after dealing with the case of Abdul Ghaffar Khan.
I have described Abdul Ghaffar Khan as preaching a gospel of discontent and of resistance to Government because I have arrived at this conclusion from his writings, from the general tenour of his public speeches of last year, and finally from statements or remarks or speeches made by others after attending him in his visits to villages in the different districts of this Province.I think that all these give us a much truer indication of what is going on than the report of the Tahsildar, Mardan, regarding Abdul Ghaffar Khan's interviews with gentlemen of Status before whom he would not dare to express his true views, and to whom he would never disclose his plans. Even in the reports to which D.C. has referred as disclosing no definitely objectionable activity the Tahsildar points out that when Abdul Ghaffar and his associates get hold of "suitable young or influential men they will touch the real question"- In other words Abdul Ghaffar's activi- ties are definitely mischievous in that they seek an opportunity to create trouble.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan is sufficiently well known reference
to the district authorities but a brief/to his history of which a full account is placed below will not be out of place- A disciple of the Haji of Turangzai, Abdul Ghaffar came to notice in 1919 during the agitation against the Rowlatt Act- He was interned but was

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p034.text:
he
released when a general amnesty was proclaimed. In 1920 he took an active part in the Khilafet and Histat movements and was again sent to jail in December 1921 in consequence of his seditious activities where he A remained till the 18th August 1924. Since his release he has continued his political activities and has estab- lished connection with political agitators of the Punjab. Youth League On the 1st September 1929 he formed the Naijawen Sons in Peshawar on the lines of that established in the Punjab. The following are the aims and objects of thes Naujawan Bharat Sabha as decided by a meeting of the Working Committee of the All India Sabha in Delhi on the 2nd February 1930:-
to shar fnement
(a) To establish a free republic of workers and
peasants in India.
(b) To inspire young men with the spirit of patriotism and sacrifice
X
X
X
X
and finally (f) to organise the workers and peasants to
attain such free republic.
In December he attended the All India National Congress Session and is reported to have undertaken propaganda work in the villages to prepare the way for civil dis- obedience.
In January he took part in the proceedings to celebrate "Independence Day" and brought 25 Volunteers with him from Utmanzai.
There can be no doubt as to his fanatical
hostility to the British Government, nor as to his alliance whose A
with those avowed intention it is to bring about a
revolution by the perversion of the masses and to overthrow the present Government.
4. We have on record many reports relating to speeches
delivered by Abdul Ghaffar. I propose only to refer to
those delivered during visits to villages during the past
4 months.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p035.text:
1-3 183
In November 1929 Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Mian Ahmad Shah, Barrister, and others toured the Frontier districts in order to make known the alma and objects of the Youth League and to establish branches throughout the Province. The deputation first visited Kohat Mian Ahmad Shah opened proceedings by speaking of the Youth League which had been established in Peshawar to serve the nation and to secure the freedom of the country - Abdul Ghaffar Khan told the meeting that the people of the province believed themselves to be free but that they were in reality slaves. They could accomplish no good while they were slaves. They would become rich when they gained their rights and the freedom of the country. He compared the treatment of Indian Sepoys with that accorded to British Soldiers and pointed out that the latter were each paid 100 rupees per mensem as compared with 20 given to the Indian soldier who worked harder. That they were given free rations and two-storeyed bungalows to live in out of money belonging to India.
The meeting at Kohat was followed by a meeting at Thall and the lectures were varied to suit the different audience. The first speaker told the audience that in former days the Afghans had occupied the country from Herat to Kashmir. The country was fertile, the people were strong and hard working but yet were the poorest of all nations, and the cause of their poverty was disunion, bloodshed and lack of education. Mian Ahmad Shah told them that the Frontier Youth League had been formed to demand for the Afghan nation their lawful rights. Abdul Ghaffar Khan spoke in support. Had the Youth League been in existence before trouble arose in Afghanistan there would have been no change in regime in Kabul. Amanullah desired to raise the status of his nation but the enemies of Islam feared the civilisation of the Afghans and the loss of their trade that would ensue. For this reason Amanullah was declared to be a "Kafir" and the country was ruined.
On the 19th November the party visited Naryab and

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p036.text:
ushinawri and meetings were held in the Mosques. In
the ne
Calion
nd
bona
Shinawri Mian Armad Shah exhortel the people to unite and to demand those rights from Government which were agment demanded by the rest of India. He asked the young men to organise and join a Youth League and told them that they would receive a monthly magazine in Pushto from Utmanzai from which they could gather information of all that was going on in the Province. Abdul Ghaffar Khan discussed Afghan affairs and warned the people to be on their guard against other nations.
༡/
The Pushto magazine referred to presumably is the "Pukhtun published by Abdul Ghaffar Khan, extracts and translations of which are placed below.
In Bannu Abdul Ghaffar Khan again spoke of the difference in treatment accoriei to Indian and English Soldiers. He compared the habits of the people (of the Frontier) who could not distinguish between friend and foe and killei one another on the alightest pretext, with those of the representatives of the Government' who never fought among themselves or disclosed their secrete to our party. He compared the people to a flock of sheep without a shepherd.
Another speaker welt upon the advantages of unity. He gave as an example the success achieved by the Muslims of Lahore in forcing Government to hand over the body of Ilam Din against its alones for birtal in Lahore, after he had been hangel for the murder of Rajpal who hai defamed the Holy Prophet.
In Dera Ismail Khan the public were told to assist the Congress in its work; that they were slaves and should strive for freedom.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan spoke of Jatieira Nath Das who had sacrificed his life for the nation and country. Muslims should have sacrificed their lives in obellence to the injuctions of the Koran but it had been left to a Hindu to furnish the example. He then referred to the Police in England and in India. In England where the

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p037.text:
Police were meant for themselves (1.0. presumably, were organised in the interests of the English) they were well paid. In India they were miserably paid and were forced to dishonesty. The polley of Government in this matter was deliberate. It under paid its servants and forced Indian officials to take bribes from Indiana in order to create 111-feeling and hatred among Indians. The public in this way were induced to belive theat Indians were bribe-takers and bad officers while the English were
not.
No dog bends down in salutation to another, yet in India one human being bows down before another. In Inila the food and accommodation provided (for an Indian) in jail are inadequate and disgraceful, but a European finds jail a veritable home. The question is one of black and white - is Any one with a black face/letested. The English are the enemies of the Indians- they want to ruin Indians and to
keep them in bondage.
Amir Habibullah had no aeroplanes and his relations with the British were cordial Amanullah bought aeroplanes and tried to reform his country; he was called a Kafir and was ruined.
During their tour there were other meetings but the extracts quoted above speak for them-selves. If the lecture were at times above the heads of village audiences the intention of the speakers cannot be mistaken. Hatred of Government, and the desire to inspire hatred are sufficient ly evident- The object of unity is not directed to the removal of ignorance or social evils but is directed against Government. The object of the speaker was to create discontent and to inspire resistance to Government. Clear as this object is in these speeches it is still more apparent in the articles published in the "Pukhtun copies of which have been promised to the villagers of Kohat- and which are probably in circulation in Peshawar district- I have placed a marked copy of a Press note below. siving extracts from this paper and there is no necessity

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p038.text:
to quote them at length.
6. I have referred in para 2 to remarks (or speeches) made by persons who have attended meetings addressed by Abdul Ghaffar Khan, repeating the latter's remarks and embroidering upon them. A dangerous example is that reported in the Confidential diary of the Superintendent of Police, Kohat, dated the 1st February 1930, when 3az1 Mahmud Shah of Darsamand told the villagers that Goverment had taken possession of the "Mazri which was Government property. That if they failed to protest they would lose their rights and he suggested referring the matter to Abdul Ghaffar Khen.
The suggestion that Government is interfering with to the possession of "Mazri" is calculated/rouse discontent. The further suggestion that the villagers should "show their power" in order to escape payment for additional Police is still more dangerous.
This is a definite instance of the fruit of Abdul Ghaffar Khan's lectures in the villages.
I have dealt with public speeches and writings which I think fully sustain my statement that Abdul Ghaf far Khan is engaged in preaching a gospel of discontent and of resistance to Government. Since the beginning of January he has been preparing the ground for Gandhi's campaign of In this matter he is on more dangerou civil disobedience. ground than in his earlier propaganda on behalf of the Youth League, or of Congress itself, and his speeches have necessarily been veiled. I place below a brief account of the speeches delivered in the Mardan and Swabi Tehsils prepared from the notes of the Sub-Inspector who accompan- ied the party. It cannot be believed that Abdul Ghaffar Khan and his associates restrict their talk to villagers to occasions on which a Sub-Inspector of Police is present to report their utierances- With the full facts of Abdul Ghaffar Khan's hicory before us and the record of his utterances in the last months of 1929 is there any justif cation for permitting him to continue his village tours

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p039.text:
and his endeavour to corrupt the countryside? It may be accepted that Lanbardars, Khans, and persons who have something to lose and whose interests are bound up with those of Government are not likely to be corrupted- but they are not the persons to whom the mission is addressed and from whom Abdul Ghafar Khan hopes to obtain support when Gandhi's programme of civil dis- obedience is extended to this Province.
8. An examination of the methods adopted in 1919 and indicate on other occasions of revolutionary activity will/the methods which we may anticipate to be adopted in the present day. The leaders of any movement against Govern- ment are powerless unless the masses can be roused. In 1907 the provisions of the Colonisation/Bill were utilised to make the peasants believe that Government contemplated oppressive measures against them. In 1919 the Rowlatt Act sufficed to provide rumours of unlimited powers to be granted to the Police to effect oppression. No speeches manner were made in the open to explain in detail the in which the Police were to receive these powers. The Bill was publicly condemned and in private rumour was set afloat, after which Gandhi's message was pronounced "Commit no violence"- and was construed in its true meaning.
In 1923 when the Babbar Akali trouble broke out in the Punjab and the administration of two districts was paralysed it was directly due to the delivery of speeches in the villages denouncing Government for oppression, for impoverishing India and for innumerable acts of tyranny, accompanied by incitement to resist oppression by violence.
On all these occasions trouble ensued owing to inactior at the outset against the organisers of the movements.
No counter propaganda on the part of Government among among villagers or parents of youths who are being led
astray is of any avail, and experience has established

9. At present we are experiencing combined and detere mined action to bring Government into contempt and hatred accompanied by a tentative movement to prepare the villagers for action. The movement may be due to Hindu inspiration as it was in 1919, but it is not for that reason to be lightly disregarded, nor can we assume that the leaders are without influence, nor that their efforts will certainly fail. The worst revolutionary papers are being brought into the Province from the Punjab by Milap Singh, Vice President of the City Congress Congress Committee, member of the Provincial Committee, and associate of Abdul Ghaffar Khan, and are finding aready sale; Peshawar has now its own revolutionary organ, assisted from the Punjab, organised & managed by members of the Naujawan Sabha, fellow workers of Milap Singh and Abdul Ghaffar Khan, who are both members of the Provincial Congress Committee; finally Abdul Ghaffar Khan is publishing a paper in Pushto in his village, conveying the same teachings, and he is engeged simultaneously in propaganda in the villages, ostensibly advocating the formation of "Panchayats", preaching unity a suggesting a better system of education to cure the Pathan of his ignorance. To what end? The report of the Tehsildar Mardan furnishes the reply and further corrobo- ration, if needed, will be found in the report regarding the private meeting of the Provincial Congress Committee held on the 30th January 1930.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p056.text:
As many false stories are being spread by persons who desire to cast discord between the Sirkar and the riyaya listen, 0 people of the North-West Frontier Province, to the true story of the disturbances of April 23rd. in Peshawar City. On that day 12 Congress- walas of the City had been arrested by the Police for sedition against the Government. A crowd collected and made a disturbance. The Deputy Commissioner went to the spot to prevent a breach of the peace. The mob then killed a British soldier and attacked the Deputy Commis- sioner and his companions with stones and bricks, axes and lathies, wounding the Deputy Commissioner and other Government servants. They also set fire to a motor car with the object of burning to death the people inside. Then and not till then the soldiers opened fire to save their lives and the lives of other Government servants. What Government in any country would not fire shots in such a case ?
It is those interested persons who for their own object mislead others who are in fact responsible for the deaths that have occurred. Government will not use force against its subjects unless compelled, and all the wounded who have been brought to hospital are being given every care and attention. Do not be misled by lies but judge for yourselves.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p058.text:
The true Congress campaign, namely a demand for Independence and its attainment by passive and non-violent resistance to authority and by the breach of selected laws by the Hindu element has little genuine support in this Province.
Coincident, however, with the declaration of Indepen dence and the initiation of the Congress Campaign there has been a series of circumstances which has created a sense of unrest and which has aroused a miscellaneous collection of people and has attached them to the Congress party as tools to resist authority, and these people are not being utilised by our local Congress Committees to further their own ends and to bring about an apparent support of the Congress Campaign. This apparent support is really the outbreak of a spirit of lawlessness, which is always existent and easy to rouse in this province and it is being developed in racial antagonism of Black versus White.
The Hindu element of Peshawar is dissatisfied with its lack of share in Government service and may be said to be in sympathy with any movement that may accelerate reforms to its personal advantage. There are no Hindus of influence or position in the Congress party in Peshawar, and the most active are the professional agitators of the needy middle and lower classes. The Congress Committee has been established by the latter with some support from down country and the party obtains a show of sympathy and assistance from its own community in Peshawar and in the towns of the Province either in consequence of intimidation or from a sense of fear of injuring the interests of the community elsewhere in India.
The Congress party has won over many of the Khilafat- ists and a proportion of the more fanatical Muhammadans by utilising agitation against the Sarda Act, and this legal measure is at present the cause of bitter resentment against Government as an unjustifiable piece of inter- ference by the white man, in the religion of Islam. Even

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p059.text:
Muhammadans who hold aloof from the Congress have boon embittered by this piece of legislation.
The Naujawan Bharat Sabha of the Punjab established and subsidised a branch of the society in Peshawar in the autumn of last year. This branch has been very active in importing the most scurrilous and revolutionary newspapers from the Punjab and in disseminating revolutionary matter of its own manufacture, sometimes simultaneously with Congress propaganda and sometimes independently. This society has incited and roused all the lowest Muhammadan classes in Peshawar who have nothing to lose and everything to gain by disorder.
Finally the Congress Party has attached to itself a few Muslim barristers, lawyers and small landowners of the type of Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai who are political firebrands and who, while professing adherence to the Congress creed, have been simultaneously engaged in pushing on revolutionary propaganda for the sake of notoriety and personal profit. The rural population has also been accessible to the agitator owing to increase of revenue at the recent settlement and a succession of bad harvests, which have produced heavy arrears.
The result of these simultaneous anti-British and anti-Government movements has been the creation of acute racial bitterness among the urban population and of a strong element of disorder which is being used by a small party of Hindus to further the Congress Campaign. The feeling of racial animosity was particularly marked in the riots of the 23rd. April. The Congress creed alone would find little support from the Hindus of the province alone and negligible support from the Muhammadans in the absence of any genuine grievance, or of ill-feeling worked up through the platform and the press.
The support now being given by the public is considerable. It extends beyond Peshawar City into the
district into parts of Charsadda and Mardan sub-divisions.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p060.text:
The causes have been partially explained in para.1 above. The suppression, or continuance and growth of the spirit of lawlessness and racial hatred will depend on the measures now taken to deal with it. Action will be necessary against each of the elements which have contributed to bring about the present state of affairs, and each will require differentiation in treatment. As a single remedy in regard to the Muhammadan situation it is suggested that the immediate repeal or non-application of the Sarda Act to the Muhammadans of this province would do much to allay Muharmadan feeling.
The widespread opposition to authority is due to the persistent incitement of the masses against Government by the vernacular press. The repeal of the Press Act gave an impetus to scurrilous writing, which it has been impossible to check. The weapon of proscription has not merely been a failure, but has done much to aggravate the evil which it was intended to suppress. It has accorded notoriety to the worst type of journal, has given a fillip to its sale and has encouraged a repetition of the offence in a worse form. The license accorded to the press has been similarly extended to public speeches and incitement to violence has more often been ignored than prosecuted. This province was formerly, little affacted by political or revolutionary activity but the passing of the Sarda Act, followed by an All-India Muhammadan agitation, has created disaffection, while the importation of seditious newspapers from the Punjab has created a taste among the public for such matter and has led finally to the publication of similar newspapers in this province, and to the rise of orators who model their speeches on the writings and speeches of the neighbouring province.
This province has also been adversely affected by the visits of emissaries from the Punjab, and by return visits of our budding revolutionaries.

On that day 12 Congress- walas of the City had been arrested by the Police for sedition against the Government. A crowd collected and made a disturbance. The Deputy Commissioner went to the spot to prevent a breach of the peace. The mob then killed a British soldier and attacked the Deputy Commis- sioner and his companions with stones and bricks, azes and lathies, wounding the Deputy Commissioner and other Government servants. They also set fire to a motor car with the object of burning to death the people inside. Then and not till then the soldiers opened fire to save their lives and the lives of other Government servants. What Government in any country would not fire shots in such a case ?
It is those interested persons who for their own object mislead others who are in fact responsible for the deaths that have occurred. Government will not use force against its subjects unless compelled, and all the wounded who have been brought to hospital are being given every care and attention. Do not be misled by lies but judge for yourselves.

mwed his party as the "Radical Congressmen's League". SUBHAS CHARRA 206# was unanimously elected as the President of a Provisional Committee, who would select his own personal Secretary. Five secretaries are to be selected, one from Bombay and Madras, one from the C.P., and Bihar, one from the Punjab and Delhi, one from Bengal, Orissa and Ass and one from Sind and North Western Provinces. SUBHAS will try to mobilise all the Leftist forces all over india. There will be two organisers for each Province. This party will start five papers, one from Bengal, one from Bihar, one from Delhi, one from Bembey, and one from South India. Messre. HARIMAN, ANEY, Professor RANGA and SUBHAS CHABRA BOSE would work together. SARKAR LAL and others will finance the party. The aims and objects of this party are the same as reported in my statement dated 27/28.4.39 under the heading "Congress Radioal Party".

(of) Association should be similarly liable (to for- feiture whenever) found whether owned by it or not.
3. The advantages anticipated from main proposals
are
Firstly. seizure of Headquarters and
moveable
properties would have definite disturbing effect on organisation.
Would
Secondly. Occupation of Headquarters by the Government/ be visible demonstration of intention to deal firmly with movement and moral effect would be considerable; if buildings were used for Government purposes such as accomodating tropps or the Police effect would be increased. Particular illustrations are Congress House on Bombay and Ashrams in Gujrat which are centras of Civil Disobedience activities.
4. On the other hand the following points require careful consideration.
Firstly the question of compensation to third party. Government of India are clear that no compensation should le given to third party who leases the premises to association after it has been declared unlawful. In regard to prior lease Government on occupation could take over liability for rent or after seizure could hand it back to owner on definite conditions. Government of India as at present advised are inclined to think that Ordinance should be so worded as to allow both these proces dures but it is a matter for consideration whether Ordinance should m specifically give any claim for compensation or leave it as a matter of grace.
Secondly. Complications may arise in case buildir belong to village community or which have religious associations but local Governments would have discret to proceed against these or not as seems expedient. Thirdly. There is danger that unlawful asso
Al

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p067.text:
Раде
(Associatione) might establish themselves in. temples or othe other religious buildings in order to defeat object of Ordinance. Government of India would particularly like to know if Local Goverments consider this contingency probable and if so whether it will not be serious embarrassment.
In regard to supplementary proposals mentioned in paragraph two above Government of India consider it doubt- ful whether in practice it would be possible to lay hands. on funds of any great value but possession of powers might make activities of associations more difficult. The power to seize moveable properties whenever found t would have deterrent effect on third parties assisting association which for instance would find it difficult to obtain hired means of transport.
Views of officers regarding firstly question of policy, secondly scope of powers to be taken, thirdly specific points mentioned above, should be communicated to Government of India at very early date.
a

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p069.text:
Governments that may assist in counteracting the activities of unlawful associations. The power to seize and confiscate funds is in itself a great deterrent to the raising of subscriptions from the public.
4. No compensation should be given to a third party who leases premises to an association that is declared unlawful. Responsibility should devolve on house-owners in the matter of leasing their property to persons engaged in revolutionary activity, and there should be no liability for rent against Government or for any form of compensation. Restoration of house property to a third party should be subject to specific conditions.
The action taken in this province has not had the effect of unlawful associations obtaining refuge in temples, or other religious buildings, and the likelihood of this contingency arising appears to be remote in this province.
The passage of the ordinance will bring into question the validity of action taken in the North- West Frontier Province. It is therefore desirable to bring our actions to the notice of the Government of India and to ask for any protection that may be
necessary.
I have the honour to be,
Sir,
Your most obedient servant,

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p074.text:
A note on the present "Independence Movement in
the North West Frontier Province.
1. This movement in the Province may be said to begin with the formation of the Frontier Youth League or Afghan Jirgah at Utmanzai in September 1929. The League became connected with the Congress, the Naujawan Sabha and the Khilafat Movements through the association of their leaders. The leaders were present at the Lahore Congress in December, on Independence Day January 26th in Peshawar and at the great meeting of the Youth League at Utmanzai on April 19-20.
2. The declared object of the League was the attain- ment of complete independence. It promised to work for Hindu-Muslim unity, the betterment of the condition of the people and the political education of Youth. The President was Abdul Akbar, of Umarzai, a Hijrat refugee trained in Tashkent. The Founder was Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai.
3. The League was advertised by the frequent tours of Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Mian Ahmad Shah, Secretary, some- times accompanied by Sayad Lal Badshah, Secretary Khilafat Committee and Faira Khan, Secretary Congress Committee. At the meeting at Utmanzai it claimed to have established 67 branches and to have enlisted 835 Khudai Khidmatgarans in the Province besides from
500 to 600 locally in Charsadda.
4. Although the leaders were themselves working in close association, it was their policy to keep the membership of their Leagues and Sabhas separate until the time for concerted action should arrive. Before that time however the arrests of nearly all the leaders were made and the intense agitation which followed resulted in some form of civil disobedience being put into practice, before its organization was perfect.
The "Khudai Khidmatgaran" now quite outgrew their

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p075.text:
161 original function of a select band of volunteers under the executive of the Youth League, and ite uniform was worn by every villager who felt sympathy for any cause or discontent. Numbers of villagers now wearing red clothes are not associated with any League or Sabha. They have merely embraced that part of the League's Programme which professes to work for the good of the whole people.
are
6. Two members of the Youth League, Mian Jaffar Shah and Mian Abdullah Shah, who succeeded Abdul Ghaffar Khan, have now published an announcement that the League will Support the whole Congress Programme. These leaders living away from Charsadda, not to evade arrest but because they have lost control of the popular movement which has now grown up.

To

- Polindia, Simla.
Your No.1679-C of the 25th.May.
The following
propaganda newspapers were published in Peshawar by the Naujawan Bharat Sabha and distributed throughout the Province:-
"Naujawan Sarhad" from January 20 (monthly) Naujawan Sarfarosh"-from March 30 weekly) Paigham-i-Jang"- from April '30 (weekly) All these newspapers were under the management of Abdur Rahman Riya of Peshawar. They all bear the Hammer and Sick le emblem and display such phrases as "Liberty or Death", "Long Live Revolution!", "Equality, Fraternity and Freedom!" They printed Bolshevik articles reproduced from the Indian press. The Secretary of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha, Sanobar Hussain, now under arrest, was himself a villager of of Kagawala, near Peshawar, and another member was S Saiyad Amir Shah of Moosazai, near Peshawar. The latter was a Hijrat refugee trained in the Tashkent School in 1920. Both these men are office-bearers in the Khalil and Mohmand Zamindars' Association. In this association 'Zamindar' means a small owner who cultivates his own land.
The Naujawan Bharat Sabha and the Association last mentioned are both connected through their leaders with the Provincial Congress and the Frontier Youth League of Charsadda. The President of the Youth League was Abdul Akbar, also under arrest, another Hijrat refugee who was also trained in Tashkent. The founder of the Frontier Youth League, Khan Abdul Ghaffar Khan, was also Vice President of the Provincial Congress. Although in March in a letter to the "Muslim Outlook" it was claimed by its founder, Abdul Ghaffar Khan, that the Frontier Youth League had nothing in common with the Naujawan Bharat Sabha, still on the 19th. April at a meeting at Utmanzai, near Charsadda, ? the Frontier Youth League, the Provincial Congress, the Naujawan Bharat Sabha and the Provincial Khilafat Committee all met on a common platform. announced that the Frontier Youth League had 67 Branches throughout the Province.
The rules of the Frontier Youth League declare the object of the League to be the demand of complete indepen- dence. The League consisted of a Provincial Central Commit- tee of 40 persons, an executive of 10 persons, and controlled district branches, tahsil branches and village branches. The rules do not provide for volunteers.J
In Abdul Ghaffar Khan's paper, the "Pakhtun" of April, the
irfani alin Wan Secretary of the "Khuda-i-Khidmatgars" Association states

the duties of volunteers is the care of Islam and the service of the nation. This volunteer association has ? grown up inside the Frontier Youth League and eventually has swallowed up its whole village membership. In April it was claimed that there were about 600 of these volun- teers in the Charsadda Tahsil and about the same number in other parts of the province. Since then the numbers have considerably increased and the latest number claimed by the Association is 20,000. Saiyad Qaim Shah, writing in the "Tribune" of May the 5th. explainedthe place held by the "Khuda-i-Khidmatgars" or "Red Shirt Association" in the Afghan Jirga, which is the last name given to the Frontier Youth League. He disclaims all taint of Bolshevism and claims that the "Khuda-i-Khidmatgars" are non-violent under oath in the pursuit of social reform. He explains the choice of a red uniform as an accident.
With this note are attached specimens of three news- papers with their advertisements, and a specimen of the badge worne by the volunteers of the Naujawan Bharat Sabha.
Peshawar, 10-6-30.
Deputy I.G.Police, C.I.D.,N.-W.F.P

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p078.text:
Haji ABDUL GHAFFAR KHAN of Utmanzai (Peshawar Dist.).
Abdul Ghaffar Khan, son of Bahram Khan
(B.1889), was educated at the Peshawar Mission School
until 1920. He then came under the influence of the
Haji of Turangzai and was Secretary of the Haji's Islamia School at Utmanzai to which he subscribed largely.
When the Haji Sahib of Turangzai fled from India, Abdul Ghaffar Khan did his best to effect a reconciliation between the Haji and Government.
Abdul Ghaffar first appears as an agitator against the Rowlatt Act in 1919. In May he fled to the Haji's camp in Independent Territory and was brought back by his father. He was interned until November when he furnished security under Section 40 F.C.R. In 1920 he led a party of Muhajarin to Afghanistan through Mohmand Country and returned to his home in November. He established an Azad School at Utmanzai and became President of the Peshawar Khilafat Committee. He was arrested in December 1921 and remained in Jail for three years having refused to furnish security under Section 40 F.C.R. The evidence against him was his presence at a meeting in Lahore where 'jehad' was preached.
In February 1925 he joined the 'Tanzim' movement of Dr. Kitchlew and Maulvi Zaffar Ali, and made a tour of the Province. At the of the year he became Vice Presiden of the Peshawar Congress Committee under Dr.C.C.Ghosh. Between May and October 1926 he performed the 'Haj'. In December 1927 he addressed the All-India Jamiat-ul- Ulema Conference at Peshawar.
On the 3rd. February 1928 he presided at a public meeting when it was resolved to boycott the Simon Commission. In May he started his monthly nationalist magazine "the Pakhtun". In June he applied for passports for himself and his sons to go to Kabul to join the Habibia College of King Amanullah. In November he became Vice President
E

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p079.text:
of the Frontier Provincial Congress Committee and a member of the Punjab Khilafat Committes.
He was one selected by the A11-India Khilafat Conference for the peace mission to the Shinwaris. In December be attended the 411-India National Congress in Calcutta. In June 1929 be formed the Afghan Red Crescent Society and in an appeal for funds be accused the British Government of having supported the "Bacha-1-Sagao". He visited Lanore and Delhi for the Zamindar's Amanullah Fund and endeavoured to reach Afghanistan through Quetta. He saw ex-King Aramllah in Bombay. In April 1929 he became Vice President of both the Peshawar Khilafat Committee and the Frontier Provincial Congress Committee. In September he formed the "Zalmo Jirga -the Youth League. He made a tour of the Province. In December he attended the All-India National Congress at Lahore.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p080.text:
ELLES OF THE FRONTLER YOUTH LEAGUE
(Published by Mian Ahmad Shah, Barrat-Law, Peshawar and printed at the Iqbal Steam Press, Peshawar).
Name This League will be named the "Frontier Youth League" and its official language would be Pushto, other languages, however, can be substituted at the time of necessity.
The League's office will for the present be at Utmanzai, Tehsil Charsadda.
Object. The object of the League is to attain complete Independence, and with a view to achieve this it will try (A) To effect Union between Hindus and Mussalmans, (B) To improve the religious, financial and educational condition, and to effect political awakening (among the youths of the Frontier Province).
The League will consist of:-
(A) The Provincial Central Committee, (of 40 persons).
(B) The Executive or Working Committee, of ten persons.
(c) office-bearers,
(D) General members.
Rules. The League will have 7 district branches, Mardan and Mansehra being regarded as two separate districts. The District branch will have its own. branches in every Tehsil which will also have its branches in every village. For a village branch it is necessary to have at least twenty members.
The League will hold its annual meetings at places selected by the Working Committee. The Secretary of the League will have to make arrangements for holding meetings. He will get no pay and will select some one as Assistant Secretary for himself. The President and the Secretary can spend Rs.20/- per month from the League Fund in the interests of the League.
The members of the Provincial Central Committee will pay an annual subscription of Rs.12/- and others .3/-only.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p085.text:
PUTHTUN OF UIMANZAI FOR JANUARY 1930.
There is verse in Pushto on the Title-Page
of the PUKHTUN which runs as under :-
Years of a slave in servitude is nothing As compared with a moment of freedom, even if in agonies of death.

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p086.text:
The Pukhtun Utmanzai of January 1930.
In an article Independence and India Abdul Ghaffar Khan cusses the advantages of Union. He says that union is another Those nations who have got /
me for honour, power and civilization. unity in them have got all the blessings of the world, they are far advanced in trade, agriculture and the art of governing than other nations. It is on account of unity that they are governing the other countries of the world, and will govern till the othernations have realized their worth. India has been under the control of British people for a very long time, who is to be blamed for this! It is only the Indians. No foreigner can plunder a country unless he is assisted by the people of that country. India's being in the hands of foreigners is due to the Indians themselves. Of course Indians have realised their loss but too late, and it is due to slackness on their part that their efforts are not crowned with success. Realizing their defect they have formed a big national Jerga called by the name of Indian National Congress, which is a stepping stone for the unity of Indians. A few years after the formation of the Indian National Congress, its members thought of demanding their just rights from the Government. Everybody knows when a nation over powers another, it snatches away many things which are necessary for the advancement and comfort of the conquered nation, The latter can do nothing out of its free will. This is the condition of Indians & the Frontier people. If we want to advance we must demand something from the Government. The Indians made efforts to attain their just rights, some of which have been admitted while some have been denied. The achievement of these demands which have been denied is also very essential for the Indians, and for repeating these demands thousands of respectable Indians have been sent to jail and are dooned to undergo different sorts of hardships. Inspite of all these things, there are some

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p087.text:
youths who still press hard for their just rights, telling the
Government to let us be termed Bad Mashes, but in the cause of our mother land we are quite prepared to go to prisons. Our efforts will be continued till they are fruitful. And for supporting the cause of their mother land many people have suffered, so much so that a Hindu prisioner in Lahore jail owing to bad treatment and bad rations went on hunger strike and the result was that he died. There are many other youths in the country who are prepared to undergo similiar sort of hardships. The leaders who are outside the jail are busy for obtaining their rights. They are of opinion that if we are given less than what is given to Australia and South Africa etc, we will make an announcement of freedom on the 1st January 1930.
Having seen the unity of the Indians and their prepared- ness to undergo all sorts of hardships the British Government have declared their willingness to give them their rights, provided they abondon the announcement of Independence on the 1st January 1930. What is the reason of all this? It is the unity of Indians and their determinations to make sacrifices for their mother land. There was a time when the British Government used to abhore our leaders and send them to jails, but now they come forward and request our leaders to wait a little till to get their rights.
ין
As an example we make a reference to the speech of the Secretary of State for India delivered by him on the 18th December 1929 in the House of Parliament and show to our nation how the British people yeild before unity. The Secretary of State says that he has got a great regard for the rights of Indians and it is hoped that the opinion expressed in the press regarding the situation in India by some irresponsible Indians must not be believed by the British people in England.
Secondly the central Government is in consultation with

Oh Pathan! Come and know thyself.
Show your position to the world.
You know nothing about yourself and about the world. Like an ass you are happy on the heap of rubbish.
You are proud without possessing any wealth or commanding any power. Who will be more foolish than you in this world?
You have not learnt self-respect from any body;
And you know nothing as well about Faith.
To salam others is thy lot,
And in this you do not see your degradation.
Like a woman you have to serve some one or other,

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p092.text:
The PUTUN (UTMANZAI) for March 1930.
ADDRESS TO THE PATHANG.
Oh Pathan! Come and know thyself.
Show your position to the world.
You know nothing about yourself and about the world. Like an ass you are happy on the heap of rubbish.
You are proud without possessing any wealth or commanding any power. Who will be more foolish than you in this world?
You have not learnt self-respect from any body;
And you know nothing as well about Faith.
To salam others is thy lot,
And in this you do not see your degradation.
Like a woman you have to serve some one or other,
Some time Sikh, Some time Moghal and now the Englishmen.
You dont possess conscience and are like a he-buffalo, the sons of Adam have been ruling you.
If you become an elephant, you cannot become a champion, As long as some one is sitting upon your neck (As long as you are in serfdom).
You please yourself with a fictitious honour,
This all is only a sort of satisfaction for lies.
When you are powerless and under the control of aliens, The sons of Adam do not count you among human beings.
You have been decieved with the names of respect, Have you ever tested your strength?

khudai_khidmatgar_1946_p093.text:
When out of fear you are trembling in the presence of allens.
This all shows your honour and strength.
Why the world will not call you an animal,
When you consider honour in complying with the orders of
others.
You seem like a dog to the world with a collar,
No matter whether you are a Major, or a Subedar, or a
Captain.
If you entertain any doubt in this statement, Verify it through the people of Japan.
The title of Khan Bahadurship suits only those,
who are free and have control over themselves.
It is a sort of insult (Lit: laughing) to confer the title of Khan Bahadur-ship upon a slave, Because in servitude there exists neither Khan nor a Bahadur (Brave man).
(FROM the pen of SADIQ).
My Pathan Brethren! Take lesson from the poem of Sadiq. Every Pathan should ponder over it. And in my opinion those who understands the meaning of the poem, should make others understand in Hujras and mosques.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan.

With the present disturbed tate of things among the tribe en across the border it .s difficult to stick to the stronger course to bring round the people by vigorous enforcement of the law or by the use of military. It is easier to deal with them in a softer spirit as they do not profess hostility towards the British government as they have shown in their publications. The temporary situation brought about by enforcement of the civil disobedience compaign simultaneously throughout the country is coming down as All India Reports indicate. The best course therefore is to pacify the public on their local greivances supposed or it are real. In a political crisis like the present, deis unwise to drive the opposition to hostility xikk when they profess that hostility is their objective. It may be said in favour of the public that bad economic conditions exist which have aroused ill-feeling. The public can be told in the announcement for the extension of reforms that it will open to them in the new constitution to consider the use- and fulness or otherwise of the local laws to work constitution- ally for any changes that they may consider necessary.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p010.text:
Police Department
Confidential
1931
Special Diary, D/- 20-7-31.
Peshawar District.
TELLI
A meeting of the City red-shirts was held in the Katanger mosque at about 10-30 p.m. on 18-7-31. The audi- ence numbered about 80-90. Maulvi Nazam, son of Mujahid- ud-Din of Prang, delivered a speech in Pushto saying that the Muslims had suffered a lot of troubles during the past He also advocated unity and appealed to the women to use Charkha and wear Khaddar.
Gul Ahmad 'Captain' of the red-shirts of Lahori gate Peshawar City, read out a Poem on unity etc.
The meeting ended at about 1 a.m. the following
morning.
A procession of the City red-shirts of Lahori and Gunj gates was taken out at about 8 a.m. on 19-7-31, from Chirwa Koban mohalla. The procession paraded the main bazaars and streets of the City till about 10 a.m. The processionists raise the cries of "Abdul Ghaffar Khan Zinda Bad", etc., en-route.
21 JUL 1307
1D.C., Peshawar. . D.I.G., C.I.D.
NO: 130/Cily/81
Superintendent of Police, Peshawar.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p100.text:
On the evening of the 19th instant red-shirts of "B" Division headed by Khan Mohd, General and imam Din Secretary went round the shops of Atta Sellers of the various Bazars of the City taking down the names of the shop keepers with number of the shops and asked them to sell Atta at cheap rates as the wheat is cheap in the rural areas. It is given out by the red-shirts that inpase the Atta sellers did not agree to their request their shops will be picketted.
In this connection rumour is afloat in the City that the red-shirts would start picketing over the Lorries conveying wheat to nabul from Peshawar City. Note Wheat in Charsadda and Mardan Sub Divisions is stated to be 3 rupees per Maund. Atta in the City is 8 seer per rupee.
Dann Ram
21.12.31

Intelligence Branch, N.-.2.2.
According to Fazal Din 'Sadar' of red shirts Mohalla Candiwera Illaqua Yakatut, Peshawar City advice has come from Utmanzai received in Peshawar by Bhai Jan Compounder of Dr. Khan Sahib that picketing should be discontinued. Fazal Din told his men that he would obtain further instructions through Bhai Jan.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p012.text:
Police Department. Confidential
1931
Special Diary, D/- 23-7-31.
Peshawar District.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai arrived here from Lahore by the Frontier Mail on 22-7-31. He was accompanied by Khurshid Bhen, a grand daughter of the late Sir Naurozji Dadabhai of Bombay. They put up with Dr: Khan Sahib in the Cantonments.
On the following morning (today) about 400 or 500 City Red-shirts collected in the Kissa Khani Bazar to receive Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Khurshid Bhen. At about 9 a.m. - Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Khurshid Bhen and Dr: Khan Sahib en- tered the City from the Kabuli gate and were saluted by the Red-shirts who were standing in lines on the both side of the road. Cries of "Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Abdul Ghaffar Khan", and "Sarhad aur Hindustan Azad were raised.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan and his companions then then went to the dispensary of Dr: Khan Sahib followed by a crowd of Red-shirts shouting "Long Live Revolution", etc.
1. D.C., Peshawar.
words
Superintendent of Police,
Peshawar.
2. D.I.G., C.I.D.
No:130-City/S.B., dated 23rd: July 1931.
BRAN
2J JUL 1931

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p013.text:
1830. Aly
Abdul Ghafar Khan with
Khurshed Ben a lady Congress
Worker arrived here last evening by the Frontier Mail. At the station he was received by Dr: Khan Sahib & Kian Abdul Azisz of Qazi Khel. They stayed the night with Dr: Khan Sahib in the Cantonement.
Early this morning (23. 7. 31) the hand cart drivers of Chowk Yadgar, Ninak landa, Pipal Handi & red shirts of Moti Mohalla, Bhana Mari, Gold Smiths, Sabzi Firoshan lined the road from Chirva Kiban to the Edwardes Gute on bither side to receive Abdul Ghafar Khan at the Edwardes Gate, not allowing the pedestrians to pass through the road till Abdul Ghafar Khan has passed.
At about 9 A. 1. Abdul Ghafar Khan & the lady Congress Worker drove to the city in tanga. At the Edvrades Gate he was received ovation by the Reds. Usual revolutionary cries were raised. Abdul Ghafar Khan went to the office in the Chirva Koban. The Reds followed him there. They paid plocis a general salute under the office & went to their vina.
Dhami Rain 23.7.31

Police Department. Confidential
1931.
Peshawar District,
Special Diary, D/- 27-7-31.
After the Juma prayers held in the mosque of Mohabat Khan on 24-7-31, a Maulvi of Sheikhan village addressed the audience saying that the Government was exercising oppression on the Mussalmans. The Mussalmans should unite They villagers should not allow their women to visit the City where the sight of the European ladies stimulate in their minds a desire of adopting Europeang fashions which Tows were against religion. They could not unite with the had wrillan Hindus one of whom we the 'Rangila Rasul' which con- tained attacks on their religion. The 'Jamiat-ul-Ulema' had decided that the Muslims should promote unity amongst themselves. They should, therefore, act on this decision and settle their disputes etc., privately.
2... A procession of about 40-45 Khilafat volunteers was taken out at about 5 p.m. on 24-7-31. About 20-25 volunteers of the Sadat Committeo also joined them. The processionists raised the cries of "Ruin British Govern- ment", "Kashmir Barbad", "Martyrs of Kshmir Zinda Bad", etc. The procession terminated at the Khilafat Office at about 7-30 p.m.

1931.
Special Diary, D/- 27-7-31.
Peshawar District
1. Abdul Ghaffar Khan, and Khurshid Bhen, left for Charsadda at about 2 p.m. on 23-7-31.
2. Abdul Aziz, broker, of Charsadda, Shirin, Chowdhri of the 'Garibanan', and two other red-shirts, were notic- ed collecting funds in the Sabzilarra Bazar on 23-7-31. 3. About 20 to 22 red-shirts of Mohalla Nau, 'B' DVI.s Peshawar City, held a parade in Lakkar Mandi, 'B' Dvn., Peshawar City, at about 5-30 p.m. on 25-7-31, under the Command of Amir Shah and Nur Din 'Captains'. They went to Lakkar Mandi in the form a procession and returned to their office at about 7 p.m.
4. The hearing of the case against Abdul Ghafur Khan of Tehkal and his companions has been fixed for " 4-8-31.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p016.text:
Police Department.
Confidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 31-7-31.
Peshawar District.
the
Devi Dass Gandhi and Mian Ahmad Shah came back from Charsadda at about 10-30 a.m. on 30-7-31, and went to the Bungalow of Dr: Khan Sahib in the Cantonments. They then paid visits to the offices of the City Red-shirts and the Congress Committee where they held conversations with the Congress leaders. At about 1 p.m. D.D. Gandhi and Abdul Aziz, Commandants of the City Red-shirts went to Pabbi and came back at about 2-30 p.m. D.D. Gandhi then paid a visit to the house of Amir Chand Barwal where he took his food and held conversations with Lal Badshah, Pir Bakhsh, Abdul Rab, as im Jan etc. At about 4 p.m. he went to the dispensary of Dr: Khan Sahib where he was interviewed by Nikko Devi.
At about 5-15 p.m. he left for Kohat in a car with At 5 S. Ram Singh of Bannu, Mian Ahmad Shah, and another.
It is stated that from Kohat D.D. Gandhi will go to Bannu on the morning of 31-7-31 where from he will come back to Peshawar on the 1st: of August 1931, and then he and Abdul Ghaffar Khan will leave for Bombay and attend the All India Congress Committee meeting which is to be held there.
2. Dr: Khan Sahib and his wife left for Nathia Galli in a car on 29-7-31. Abdul Ghaffar Khan, who is also going to Nathiagalli to seethe Hon'ble the Chief Commis- sioner, joined them at Nowshera. They are expected back today.
1. D.I.G., C.I.D.
Mand
Superintendent of Folice, Peshawar.

1931. 53 N. W. F. Province.
Confidential Diary of A.S.P. City 30th July 1931.
At about 7-0 a.m. the Red Shirts of Nouteh assembled in uniform to attend the funeral in Tohkal of the grand mother of Abdul Ghafoor. Although they did not march in the form of a procession they all proceeded in one group and as such can be considered to have broken the order under Section 144 C. P. Code. They returned from Tehkal at about 11-0 a.m.
At about 11-0 a.m. Devi Dass with Mian Ahmad Shah arrived in the City and visited the offices of the Red Shirts and Congress Associations during the day. He met most of the leading people of these two associations during the day but did not stay for long in either office. From 1.0 to 2.30 p.m. he was absent from the City and is reported to have visited Pubbi. During the afternoon he took food with Amir Chand Banwal in Karimpura Bazaar to which place he was accompanied by a few Congress volunteers. At 5.30 p.m. he left in a car accompanied by Ahmad Shah and Ram Singh of Bannu for Kohat.
Nothing is known as yet of the result of any discussion held by Devi Dass with the Local Political
agitators but a special diary will be submitted as soon as this becomes known.
Sd/- W. Humphry. A.S.P. City."
Please call A.S.P. City's attention to my marginal remarks about the Red Shirts attending the funeral procession of Abdul Ghaffur's grand mother.
Sd/- S. E. Pears, Chief Commissioner, 4. 8. 1931.

Po ice Department. Confidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 1-8-31.
Peshawar District.
1. About 10 or 15 City red-shirts who were present in the mosque of Mahabat Khan's mosque on 31-7-31, collected funds after the Friday prayers had be said.
2. Devi Dass Gandhi, Mian Ahmad Shah, Abdul Akbar Khan of Charsadda, and Pir Shahinshah of Kohat arrived here from Kohat: at about 9-30 a.m. today.
3. Abdul Ghaffar Khan and Dr: Khan Sahib arrived here from Nathigalli on the evening of 31-7-31 and passed the night in the latter's bungalow in the Cantts. Abdul Ghaffar Khan paid a visit to the City at about 10 a.m. today.
4. Devi Dass Gandhi will take his meals at the house of Nikko Devi at 12 today.
Rom 9 8/8/31.

2. D.C.

lice Department. afidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 10-8-31.
Peshawar istrict
1. Un 8-8-31, at about 5 p.m., about 70-80 red-shirts of Bajauri Bazar, Peshawar, held a parade in Namak mandi, un- K der the command of Abdul Qayyum, their drill instructor. The parade lasted for about an hour.
2. un 9-8-31, at about 5 p.m., about 200-250 red-shirts of Chauk Yadgar, Yakkatut, Lahori Gate, Gunj, Lakkar Mandi, etc., held a parade in the Yadgar Chauk.
3. A procession of about 100-125 Red-shirts of Bajauri Bazar was taken out at about 5-15 p.m. on 9-8-31. un reach- ing Chirwakoban, the processionists saluted ur: Khan sahib and then went on to Mundi Beri. They reached radgar Chauk at at about 5-45 p.m.
ว
4. The processionists who had assembled for a parade in the Yadgar Chauk, vide para 2 above, after holding the parade, left Chauk Yadgar in a procession, under the command of Abdul Aziz, 'General' and their respective commanders, and after saluting Dr: Khan Sahib in Chirwakoban went on to Mundi Beri, Gunj, Bazar Kalan, Chauk yadgar, Bazazan Bazar, Misgaran, Egerton Hospital, Jahangirpura, Namak Mandi, Patha Mandi, Bajauri Bazar, Gulab khan, qissa Khan Bazar, end then returned to Chirwa Koban via Chauk Nasir Khan and Mundi Beri. They were joined by about 60 or 70 members of the "Sabzi-Faroshan" near Puli Pukhta who were return from Chirwa Koban after greeting ur: Khan Sahib. About 50-60 red-shirts of Moti mohalla, who were also returning from Chirwakoban after saluting Dr: khan sahib, joined the main, procession near the Egerton Hospital.
The total number of the red-shirts who took part in the procession was about 500-600. About 100-150 men in plair clothes also joined them. The processionists raised the cries of Long Live Revolution", "Hindustan, sarhad Azad",

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p024.text:
Police Department.
Confidential.
1931.
Special Diary, D/- 14-8-3).
Peshawar District.
A meeting of the Congress Committee and the red-shirts of Peshawar City, washeld at 9-30 p.m. on 13-8-31, in Namak Mandi, Peshawar city. The audience numbered about 2500-3000 including about 500-600 Red-shirts, 20-25 congress volunteers and one old Pathan woman.
The Mullah of Kurvi was in the chair. The prominent leaders present were, Abdul Ghaffar Khan, Arbab Abdul Aziz of Landi, Abdul Aziz 'General' of the red.shirts, Lal Bad- shah, qasim Jan, Abdul Rab Nashtar', Rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaz- navi', Ghulam Rabbani of the Congress Committee, Peshawar cit City, Milap Singh, political agitator, and a correspondent
of the Akali', Amritsar.
Sher Wali, à Red-shirt of Kikar mohlalla, 'B' Dvn., Peshawar City, read out a poem in Pushtu, the purport which was that they should be ready to meet any trouble in the attainment of freedom.
Nasr-Ullah of Tehkal, a Redshirt, read out a poem ir Pushto on freedom.
.61-
reveAs compel
Grment
Two Red-shirts (names not known) read out a the purport of which was that they had no fear of the machine guns and the mills of the Government and were read for any sacrifice for the sake of freedom.
Abdul Ghaffar Khan of Utmanzai, delivered a speech i Pushto saying that he was asked to propose a resolution he had no faith in resolutions as he knew that the Gove: ment did not care for them. The Government cared for po only. He said that their innocent brethren had been beat in Akbarpura village,
At this time Abdul Ghaffar Khan started weeping.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p026.text:
(3)
thrown from an aeroplane over the office of the Khudai Khid., matgars that set fire to the office burning the uniforms of the Khudai Khidmatgars and two copies of the oran. He wALB very angry that the orans had been burnt. The Government was realising water tax from them for the water which be longed to their own country. He said that he had told Maha- tama Gandhi that success could not be attained by begging. In Akbarpura people were on one side beaten and wounded, an on the other they had been arrested. The Government did not like that there should be a person on whose advice the people should act. He (Abdul Ghaffar Khan) did not like to remain abroad, but he wished to go to jail, which would happen when Mahatama Gandhi broke the truce. The Government was frightening them by her machine guns etc. but they had already had such an experience in charsadda, Utmanzai, etc. The British were terrifying the people, but they should kno that they were nothing more than the Indians in their physical structures and that the time was now over that the Indians used to fear them. The British officers were gettin high salaraies and the Indians were dying of hunger. They would take rest only when their country was free. The Government was always trying to create disunion among the different communities, but the people should be very care- ful and should ex-communicate from the societies those persons who were found propagating communalism. The Govern- ment wanted to spread disaffection among the muslims and the nindus, and by this means, to rule peacefully. she was responsible for the occurrences at campore, etc. Now it had put its eye on the Frontier Province. The Hindus, Mus- lims, Sikhs, etc., were all Khudai Khidmatgars. The Muslims should serve their Hindu brethren as the latter respected the former. Any persons who wanted to create communal questions were Government informers. The enemy (British) was after them. The Hindus, Muslims and sikhs were all oppressed and the Goverment was sucking their blood.

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p027.text:
(4)
The Pathans should help the oppressed. (On this all the gathering said they would act on his advice). He further said that he would write to Mahatama Gandhi that they were being butted by the Government. They would use the horn of Patience and not of violence. In the last struggle they used the weapon of Patience and therefore they had turned out successful. God was the enemy of the tyrant and helper of those who used Patience. If Mahatama Gandhi broke the truce then they would see what would happened. The accused in the Babra Drama Case were being oppressed in the jail. There should be no Hindu-Muslim quarrel. They should boycott the cloth made by the British who were oppressing them. He then proposed the following resolution:-
"This meeting of the public of Peshawar expresses its hated and contempt of Government action in using violence against the villagers of kbarpura and warns her that the responsibility of the result of her action would lie on her own shoulders".
Abdul Rab 'Nashtar', pleader, a member of the Congress Committee, Peshawar, made a Pushto speech saying that they should act on the instructions of the Khan. The officers the Frontier had tried their best, at the time when negt tions in regard to the Gandhi Irwin Pact were in progres to hinder their success, but in vain. In a recent issue of the Civil and Military Gazette it had been suggested that an agreement should be arrived at with India, but not
with the Frontier Province. The violent attitude of the Government was illegal. Cases of violence were taking place in other parts of India, but none such case had occured in the Frontier. The Government wished the people of the Frontier to resort to violence. The Government, inorder to derive advantage from India, wanted to keep the Frontier - slavery. India could not remain free as long as the Fron- tier Province was in slavery. They should act according the principles of 'Non-violence', and should use country- made articles only. They should keep unity amongst themsel
еб

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p028.text:
(5)
themselves as efforts were being made to divide them. In con clusion he supported the above resolution.
The Mullah of Kurvi delivered a short speech saying that the Government had practised repression in Akbarpura where the people, including old men and small children, had been beaten and wounded. The number of wound od was about 180 and 10 of the villagers had been sent to the jail. The only rema- dy for all such troubles was Patience. They should maintain unity amongst themselves. The Government was trying to sup- press the red-shirt movement.
The meeting ended at about 11-15 p.m. with cries of "Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Abdul Ghaffar Khah", etc.
1. I.G. Police, N.W.F.P.
2. D.I.G., C.I.D.
3. D.C., Peshawar.
No:130-City, dated 14-8-31.
Superintendent of Police, Peshawar.

Postewer District.
A meeting of the city Red-shirts was held near Ziarat Hazratji sahib, Dhand mohalla, "C" Dvn., Peshawar City, at about 9-30 p.m. on 14-8-31. The audience numbered about 300-350 Red-shirts. Maulvi Abdul Ghafur of Peshawar took the chair.
A party of 5 or 6 Red-shirt boys (names not known) read out a Pushto poem in praise of the 'Generals' of Tirah. and condemning their enemies. It was also said in this poem that the Khajuri plain should be vacated. The Frontier Fro- . vince and India were their countries and the British should vacate them. It also praised the red-shirts and exhorted/ people to throw off the yoke of slavery. Cries of Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Abdul Ghaffar Khan', 'Islam Burri", etc., were raised by the audience.
Maulvi Abdul Ghafur of Peshawar read out some verses from the Qoran and then said that the red colour was the right colour as the Prophet had worn a red 'Chadar'. People should start wearing Red clothes which meant that they were ready to sacrifice themselves for the sake of the Nation. He asked people to unite and keep themselves ready to face all sorts of troubles. They should not think that success lay in wearing red clothes only. He prayed to God to send the British back to their country as the people in India were sick of the British rule. At this cries of Long Live Revolurion", etc., were raised.
and
Red-shirts
A boy 6 or 7 yard then got up and said that they were not going to obey the laws of the Government and that they wanted the British to leave India. They supported the speech of Maulvi Abdul Ghafur and said that God should ruin the Devil. Sir Norman Bolton, hr: Metcalfe, and Mr: Isemonger had oppressed them and now the present civil and Police officers were oppressing them.

A meeting of the city Red-shirts was held in Moti mohalla
'B' Dvn., Peshawar City, at about 7-30 p.m. on 18-9-31. At the re- start the gathering numbered about 350-400, including about 150-200 Red-shirts. The following prominent xx% agitators
were also present:-
1. Agha Qasim Jan.
2. Rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi".
3. Chan Shah of Moti mohalla.
4. Spin Badshah of Tehkal.
5. Abdul Aziz of the city Red-shirts.
6. Qazi Amin-ul-Haq of Kurvi.
7. Abdul Aziz 'General' of the city Red-shirts.
A 'Hafiz' opened the proceedings by reading some verses from the oran.
Another Hafiz read out a Pushto poem on Independence. A young Red-shirt read out a Pushto poem the purport of which was that the faces of the informers would be blackened, and which concluded with a prayer for the liberty of the country and for Abdul Ghaffar khan to be their king. Shouts of "Amin" were raised as soon as the poem ended.
The meeting was then adjourned for the evening prayers. At about 7-45 p.m. about 200-250 Red-shirts of the city, under the command of their 'General' Abdul Aziz, and 30-40 Congress volunteers, also arrived in the meeting in process- ions.
Cries of "Long Live Revolution", "Long Live Abdul Ghaffar Khan", "Long Live Ghazi Abdul Ghafur", "Ruin Informers", etc., were raised. R
Two Mullahs recited/ "Na'at" (poems in praise of the Pro- phet).
The gathering at this time numbered about 1000/1500, in- cluding about 500-600 Red-shirts, 30-40 Congress volunteers, and 10-15 women; the last mentioned stood on one side of the gathering.

(2)
Agha Qasim Jan proposed Chan Shah for the chair, and Rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi' supported the proposal.
Chan Shah thanked the audience for the honour conferred on him.
A small Red-shirt boy read out a Pushto poem on the pre- sent backward condition of the Muhammadans.
Rahim Bakhsh "Ghaznavi" made a speech in Urdu in which he said that he was in favour of what Abdul Ghaffar Khan had given out in the meeting held in Namak Mandi on 13-8-31 1.0., that there was no use of passing resolutions etc., but as he was compelled to do so, he proposed the following resolution:- This meeting of the public of Peshawar, expresses its hatred and contempt of the Government's action in arresting and convicting Abdul Ghafur Khan, Ashiq Shah, Muqarrab khan, etc., without any fault on their part. The Government has by doing so injured the feelings of the public".
Continuing his speech he said that this action of the Government had injured the feelings of 32 crores Indians. The Sarband case was a false one and had been concocted with the object of taking revenge from the people. He would be pleased if all of them went to the jail.
Addressing Government he said that if the Delhi agree- ment was torn into pieces at Bardauli, its dust would swim in the atmosphere of the Frontier also and no trace of the af Government would be found here. To convict Abdul Ghur Khan was not an ordinary thing. All such things were trickeries of Government. The (communal) disturbances were being nursed by the agents of the Government. The question was not of 'Religion' but it was of 'Rule'. The disgracing of their re- ligion had been established the day on which the British troubles of gained power in India. The Indians had been suffering/various sorts during the period of their slavery.
The object of Abdul Ghafur Khan's being thrust into the Jail was that the Pathans, who knew nothing and were igno- rant, would be excited into violence) and the Government Yould get an opportunity to cool her heart.
C
Referring to certain incidents in the Mife of the
I

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p033.text:
(3)
Prophet he remarked that Islam taught them to act on the principle of Non-violence. The Mulvis said that those who shouted 'Inqilab were 'Kafirs' but the people should not listen them on the othe hand they should take pride in shout- ing Inqilab'. He then criticised the speech of Hafiz Abdul Hannan, who, a few days back, had remarked that the Congress volunteers were 'Kafirs'.
Contninuing his speech he remarked that the Government never be effected by resolutions etc., and therefore they should do something practical. They should have discipline and organisation amongst themselves as was taught by Islam. The other communities were struggling hard for Independence and the Muslims should not lag behind them. The present situ- ation was similar to that in the time of the Prophet. At that time only a few persons hoisted the flag of Independence but now 40 crores of Muslims were unable to do what they had done -ir
If in spite of the numbers they could not do anything then their was no need of their existence. He asked the auduence if they would work for the freedom of India. All replied "Certainly". He appealed for the use of Khaddar and boycott of things manufactured by the tyrant rulers. The disturbances in Turkey, Afghanistant and other Islamic countries were due to the slavery of the Indian Muslims. If there had not been British rule in India, Aman-Ullah Khan would not have been dethroned. He said he remembered what had happened during the great war. He remembered also the cries of the orphans and the widows of those who had shed their blood in Turkey. None of the islamic countries would know peace as long as the -, (Indian) Muslims were slaves. They would enjoy the taste of le- freedom when they had liberated themselves from slavery. -ed He would not mind if, out of 35 crores, one one remins aliv but was free.
He then praised the work of the Red-shirts and said some persons were criticising Abdul Ghaffar Khan's action closing the doors of the Mohabat khan mosque for those who

He remarked they had been branded as 'Kafire' since the day they had raised their vioce against the tyrant Government. In 1930, 500 of them had been killed in the Kissa Khani Bazar, but it was an ordinary sacrifice in the cause of the freedom. Referring to the communal disturbance at Dera Ismail Khar Government had said that it had not a single shot at the rioters. This showed that the Government was trying to find an opportunity to fire at the people. In 1930, when their brtheren were killed in the Kissa Khani Bazar, he got the news in the jail and was sorry to have lost a chance to mix his blood with that of the 'Martyrs'. On the next day their was a strike in the Jail. The warders were standing with loaded guns in their hands. Hetold Agha Lal Badshah that although they were unable to share the fate of their Mrtyr brethren, they would have their shure (in the jail) and their souls would join the souls of the

tyrs'
He further observed that if the hand of an Englishmen was cut, a memorial was raised for it, but no memorial existed for the Martyrs of kissa Khani Bazar.
He asked the audience whether they had any sympathy with Abdul Ghaffar Khan, and on getting a reply in the X affirmative, said that they should wear Khaddar and use country- Asaid made articles only. At this cries of "Long Live Revolution", "Ruin Informers and their familes*, etc., were raised. some- one of the audience shouted that the wives of the informers should be defiled.
Taj Muhammad, President of the Tehkal Red-shirts, made a speech saying that he supported the above resolution, and remarked that those who called congress volunteers Kafirs were 'Kafirs' themselves. He read out some verses from the

red_shirt_6_1931_pesh_dist_p035.text:
(5)
ran in support of his observations. He asked the audience not to forget the incident of the Kissa Khani Bazar and the Jallian Walla Bagh, Amritsar. The Congress was not a new body but it had existed 1300 years ag back. At that time a handful of Muslims could face such troubles as the present 45 crores could not. They should therefore resort to Patience to which -d they owe their pass present success. The conviction of Abdul Ghafur Khan, the occurrence at Akbarpura, the running over by a motor lorry an old women in Bhanamari should not excite them into committing violence, otherwise the honour that had been attained by Abdul Ghaffar Khan would be lost. Mr Wylie had written in his report that the Pathans were like did
קל
a camel, but he as not know that besides being gifted with quality of forebearance like a camel, they were also possessors of the 'Grudge' with which that animal was gifted. The Govern- ment should not use force against them. They did not possess any weapons but they had sworn not to put to trouble any one. The object of the Government in enforcing Section 144-C.P.C. and XXXXSing other high-handed actions was to excite them into committing violent acts. They should, however, be careful and face Government with patience. Innocent persons had been sent to the Jail but they should know that the Lion and not the Jackal was shut in a cage in the zoo. He said that they should say nothing to those who were working against them as they were compelled to do so. They should not wear white clothes as long as they were slaves. He thanked the citizens for observing a 'Hartal' on 17-8-31.
The resolution put forward by Rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi' was then passed unanimously.
rahim Bakhsh 'Ghaznavi' again got up and proposed the following resolution:-
I
"This meeting of the public of Peshawar expresses its hatred of the Government's action in confiscat- ing the Arms License of Chan Shah, President of the Red-shirts of Peshawar City, without making any enquiries*.

In support of this resolution he remarked the the Government had never cared for the licensed arms from 1913
to
anim
1931, but the idea of their confiscation had struck her" in July 1931. The President should not mind the confinentiopd of of his gun. The system of confiscation and grant of arms licenses would continue until the Government was their own when their would be no persons in the country who would not poessess an arm. This impotent Government may take away all their arms. such acts on her part were cowardly. The Govt. may shed an additional two seers of blood with the arms it had confiscated from Chan Shah. He would pray to God to ruin the tyrant. 32 crores of oppressed Indians were crying at the hands of the tyrant Government. He prayed for the downfall and subversion of the tyrant Government.
Ilahi Bakhsh, member of the Congress Committee, Peshawar supported the above resolution which was passed unanimously. Sami
Qazi A-ul-Haq of Kurvi made a Pushto speech saying that the foreign rule was a disease from which they were
suffering. They had helped Government in 1857, and they were
being oppressed in return. The present Government was a queer one. In Akbarpura 180 persons had been injured for not paying the fine imposed on them, and even women who were brin water to their houses w had been disgraced. The villagers had nothing in their possession to pay the fine with. Oppression had been practised at Takkar, Gujar Garhi etc. The Government had been increasing the land revenue in every settlement and still it was not content. The Musli wanted to act on the 'Shariat' but were not allowed to do would go so. A man who was oppressed vety weeping first to his parents, then to the azi and then to the ruler. What jus- tice he would meet when the ruler was not just. he (the ruler) had enforced sec:144-C.P.C., so that no meetings spoke could be held and procession taken out. He is lies. Whet justice could be expected from him under such circumstances They should not complain before any body except God. He re-

requested the audience to hold prayers and give up bad habits, and said that the Red-shirts were favourites of God and any one who molested them was not a Muslim. The people said that Abdul Ghaffar Khan was a friend of Mahatam Gandhi, but he would say the both of these poor people were striving for the release of the oppressed from the tyrant. They were doing no bad thing. If the oppression was wr stopped, no meetings would be held. All the Government of officials were taking bribes. He concluded his speech with an appeal for the strict adherence to the teachings of the 6 Qoran, and a prayer for i release from the hands of the tyrant.
A Red-shirt read out a poem in Pushto on Liberty.
One Badshah Gul of Lahori Gate read out anoth Pushto poem which was of an extremely seditious nature.
